title: Employment tasks can move arbitrary world items
description: The commit introduces player-accessible employment task drafts. A user with a host employment contract granting AssignTasks and ManageDeliveryRoutes can add get-by-id, get-by-tag, commodity, and delivery steps using arbitrary numeric cell IDs or arbitrary item/container IDs. Finalising the task only checks the user's host authority flags; it does not constrain the source cells, destination cells, items, or containers to the employment host's stockroom/shopfront/workplace or otherwise host-owned areas. The live EmploymentWorkerAI then creates a physical movement context and advances these tasks. The task context's CanPath is not actual pathfinding and defaults to true, AvailableItems enumerates deep items in the specified cell, TryCollectTaskItem directly extracts or container-takes the item, and TryDeliverTaskItems directly inserts/puts it at the destination. The AI only waits to path when NextStepLocation returns a reachable hint; if no reachable hint is returned, it proceeds to AdvanceTask anyway. This lets a malicious or compromised in-game manager use an NPC worker as an item-moving primitive outside the intended business boundary, potentially stealing or relocating other players' or world items and bypassing normal pickup/container restrictions.

title: SMTP TLS certificate validation is disabled
description: EmailHelper now sets client.ServerCertificateValidationCallback to a delegate that always returns true. Because this is MailKit's per-client certificate validation hook, an active network attacker between the MUD server and its SMTP server can present a forged certificate when SSL/TLS is enabled. The server will still connect and then authenticate with the configured SMTP username and password, exposing SMTP credentials and queued email contents such as account recovery messages. The commit removed a global ServicePointManager callback, but replaced it with the callback that MailKit actually uses for SMTP certificate validation.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Unbounded text helpers enable player script DoS
description: The new TextUtilityFunction implementation lets script authors choose arbitrary repeat counts and pad widths. For example, a player-authored computer program can compile source like `return repeattext("A", 1000000000)` or `return padright("", 1000000000)`. Execution then attempts to construct a massive string in a single statement. Counts larger than Int32 also pass through an unchecked decimal-to-int cast in GetInteger, producing exceptions. In the computer-program executor path, statements are executed directly and only ComputerProgramWaitException is caught, so these helper-triggered allocation failures or conversion exceptions can escape normal program failure handling and disrupt the MUD process/game loop. The functions are registered without restricted contexts; Text functions use only Text/Number types, which are allowed in computer compilation, and the player-facing programming command allows players to save arbitrary source code when the programming feature is enabled.

title: Employment tasks can underpay commodity shop purchases
description: This commit adds first-class commodity merchandise and a correct weight-priced shop path, but the employment purchase resolver only uses that path when PurchaseTarget.CommodityWeight is set. The older employment merchandise and item-selector purchase paths still accept any IMerchandise, including MerchandiseType.Commodity, and return a PurchaseTarget priced with PriceForMerchandise(quantity). TryPurchase then calls CanBuy/Buy or CanBuyExact/BuyExact instead of CanBuyCommodityWeight/BuyCommodityWeight. For non-stackable commodity stock, the count-based BuyFromStockedItems path treats one commodity pile as one item, transfers the whole pile, and charges only the count price. A manager with employment purchase authority can therefore author a task such as a one-count merchandise/item purchase for commodity merchandise and buy an entire stocked commodity pile for the price configured per pricing weight, bypassing the player-facing commodity-by-weight restriction.

title: Unbounded hotel room names can crash saves
description: This commit moved hotel room state from XML/text storage into normalized EF tables. The new HotelRooms.Name column is limited to varchar(200), but the existing room add and rename commands copy StringStack.SafeRemainingArgument directly into the room name without enforcing that limit. HotelPersistenceStore then writes room.Name straight into the new DbHotelRoom record. If a hotel manager supplies a name longer than the database column allows, the periodic SaveManager.Flush will call SaveChanges and receive a database update error such as "Data too long for column 'Name'". The main loop invokes this flush without a recovery path, so in production the exception can terminate the service. The attacker needs an authenticated character authorized to manage a property/hotel, but does not need staff privileges.

title: Trial docket leaks restricted legal cases
description: This commit adds PC judge trial docket functionality. The summon and judgement paths correctly use CanManuallyJudge, which checks both CanConvict and whether the actor's enforcement authority includes the target's legal class in AccusableClasses. However, TrialDocket only calls JudgementAuthorities, which includes any jurisdiction where the actor has CanConvict, and then lists every defendant with known crimes, bail, remand, or trial effects in that jurisdiction. The output deliberately ignores visibility for the defendant description and also prints full personal names, charge IDs, charge names, dates/status, and prior conviction counts. A lower-scope PC judge/enforcer who may only judge some legal classes can therefore enumerate restricted cases for protected legal classes in the same jurisdiction.

title: Physical-float settlement can seize employee cash
description: The commit introduces executable `physicalfloat` actions. When returning or settling physical float, `TryFindCarriedCurrency` builds the set of eligible currency piles from both `context.CarriedTaskItems(actor)` and `actor.Body.HeldItems`. The latter includes normal currency the assigned employee is personally holding, not just cash that was issued by this employment task. `TrySettlePhysicalFloat` then removes those coins and credits the employer's virtual cash, and `TryReturnPhysicalFloat` can deposit/move them to the employer bank/register/container path. As a result, a manager or scheduled task with the physical-float authority can create a `physicalfloat settle all` or `return all` step that confiscates an employee/player's held money instead of only operating on task-custody currency. The fix should restrict return/settle candidates to currency piles tracked as task-custody/issued float for the current task, or otherwise verify provenance before removing or crediting coins.

title: Employment purchases can remotely buy from any shop
description: This commit changes employment `purchase` steps from audit-only to executable and allows `from <shop id|name|any>`. The resolver searches `gameworld.Shops` directly and selects stocked merchandise, but it never checks the supplier shop's `CurrentLocations`, never requires a movement/path step to the shop, and never verifies that the worker is actually at a valid shopfront. It then calls `target.Shop.Buy(...)`. The native `Shop.Buy` method assumes the caller has already selected an accessible shop: it removes the stocked item from its current container/location and gives it to the actor or drops it at the actor's current location. As a result, an authenticated player/NPC with delegated employment task purchase authority can spend employer funds to pull stocked items out of any trading shop in the world, including with the `any` selector, bypassing normal physical shop access and effectively teleporting goods to the worker.

title: Oversized scheduled-rule cooldown can crash the server
description: The commit introduces scheduled-rule authoring with user-controlled cooldown values. TryParseDuration accepts numeric values such as `1000000000000d`, parses them as decimal, and passes them directly to TimeSpan.FromDays/FromHours/FromMinutes/FromSeconds. Values above TimeSpan's supported range throw OverflowException. TrySetDraftCooldown and one-shot rule creation do not catch this exception. In production builds, PlayerConnection catches any exception during command handling, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), so an authenticated character with delegated CreateScheduledRules authority can reliably terminate the MUD process by setting an oversized scheduled-rule cooldown.

title: Specific item task selectors allow targeted item retrieval
description: The new retrieval parser accepts `*<itemId>` for `getid` tasks after only checking that the item exists globally. It does not require the task author to see the item, prove the item belongs to the employment host, or constrain the source cells to host-controlled stockrooms. Source locations can still be arbitrary numeric cell IDs. At execution time, the step matches available items by exact live item ID and then calls `TryCollectTaskItems`, which removes/collects the selected item if the assigned employee can path to the source. This turns an existing broad prototype-based logistics mechanism into targeted live-object retrieval: a player or lower-privileged staff member with delegated `AssignTasks` and `ManageDeliveryRoutes` authority who knows a cell ID and live item ID can cause an eligible worker to retrieve a specific item outside the intended employment inventory boundary.

title: Return task can move arbitrary world items
description: This commit promotes the employment logistics `return` action from deferred to executable. The command parser requires the literal word `container`, but then resolves an arbitrary `IGameItem` by item id or tag. `ReturnAssetActionStep.CanExecute` only checks that the item resolves and that the destination is pathable; it does not require the item to have an `IContainer` component, to have been previously loaded by this task, or to belong to the employment host. During execution, if the worker reaches the item's current location, the step calls `TryCollectTaskItem` on that arbitrary item and later `TryReturnContainer` drops or puts it at the chosen destination. A player with delegated employment task authority could therefore create a task such as `tasks create x return container <victim item id> to here` and have an employee retrieve and move unrelated reachable world items, including non-containers, bypassing the intended reusable-container boundary.

title: Payroll settlement mints cash without debiting funds
description: The commit adds payroll liabilities, settlement, and cash claiming. However, settlement only changes payable state to ReadyToClaim/Settled and records ledger/register entries; it never checks whether the employment host has sufficient cash or withdraws from a host cash reserve/bank account. When the employee claims a cash payable, TryGiveCash creates a fresh currency pile from the currency system and gives/drops it to the actor. Default manager authority includes CreateJobOpenings, HireEmployees, and ManagePayroll, and job opening hourly rates only require a positive value, so a player or lower-trust actor who legitimately obtains employment manager authority can create or operate high-value wage liabilities and convert them into persistent in-game currency with no backing funds. This undermines world/economy integrity and can be abused to inflate currency or transfer value out of an employment host without authorization.

title: Offering gate bypass via generic put command
description: This commit introduces OfferingReceiver as an IContainer and adds offer/burn player commands. The intended offer path calls CanOffer, which evaluates CanAcceptItem with the actor and therefore runs the configured CanOfferProg. However, the generic container path calls IContainer.CanPut, and OfferingReceiver.CanPut calls CanAcceptItem(null, item). Because CanAcceptItem explicitly skips CanOfferProg when actor is null, a normal player can bypass any actor-based offering authorization by using the existing generic put command against the offering focus. Once the item is in receiver.Contents, the new burn command only checks that the item is contained and that the focus is accessible; it does not re-run CanOfferProg before executing OnBurnProg, firing offering-burn events, and deleting or replacing the offering. This can defeat builder-authored access controls such as clan/religion/permission checks and can let unauthorized players trigger scripted world effects tied to burning offerings.

title: Non-finite burn tick can crash game loop
description: The commit adds a persistent burning spell primitive with a builder-controlled tick interval. The builder validation only checks that double.TryParse succeeds and that the value is not less than 1.0. In .NET, values such as NaN and Infinity parse successfully, and comparisons like NaN < 1.0 are false, so these non-finite values are accepted and persisted. When the spell is later cast, TickSeconds is passed into SpellBurningEffect, whose constructor calls TimeSpan.FromSeconds(Math.Max(1.0, tickSeconds)). NaN or Infinity causes TimeSpan.FromSeconds to throw. Because spell execution occurs on the main game loop, a malicious builder or compromised builder account can create a crash-on-cast spell and deny service.

title: Employment managers can seize or revoke owner contracts
description: The new common employment contract handling replaced legacy shop/stable role checks with generic contract operations. `TryTerminateContract` and `TryTerminateContractsForEmployee` only require `FireEmployees`, and then terminate any active contract, including `Manager` or `Proprietor` contracts. Directly hired `Manager` and `Proprietor` contracts are also granted `EmploymentAuthoritySet.All`, which includes `HireEmployees` and `FireEmployees`. As a result, a shop manager can fire a proprietor/owner via `shop fire <owner>` or `contracts fire #<id>`, bypassing the previous checks that blocked firing proprietors and restricted firing other managers. Stable role toggles are worse: `stable manager` and `stable proprietor` now call the generic toggle helper with no proprietor-only precheck, so any stable manager with hire authority can promote themselves or an accomplice to proprietor. This is an in-game authorization escalation and business-takeover/lockout issue rather than a server-wide code execution flaw.

title: Shop import can bind shops to arbitrary existing cells
description: The commit introduces apply-shops for untrusted legacy worldfile data. Although RunShopCommand parses the RPI rooms and the transformer can warn when a shop_vnum or store_vnum is not in the parsed room corpus, those warnings do not make the shop invalid. The baseline catalog only records all existing FutureMUD Cell.Id values, and validation accepts any positive shopfront/stockroom vnum that is present in that global set. BuildShop then stores definition.StoreVnum as StockroomCellId and definition.ShopVnum as the shopfront cell. A malicious or malformed RPI NPC shop record can therefore point shop_vnum/store_vnum at any existing cell ID in the target database, even if that cell was not imported from the supplied RPI rooms. This can attach a trading shop to unrelated existing rooms, private/player/admin locations, or an existing shopfront with a different stockroom, corrupting world state and potentially exposing unintended buy/sell interactions after the operator runs apply-shops --execute. This is especially dangerous because the room importer may assign fallback IDs when legacy vnums collide, so legacy vnum equality is not a reliable proof that a cell is the converted room.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudShopImporter.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudRoomImporter.cs

title: Unbounded astronomical event searches allow CPU DoS
description: This commit adds astronomical event lookup functions that take a caller-controlled occurrence parameter. The value is converted directly to an int and passed to AstronomicalEventService.TryFindNext without any upper limit. TryFindNext repeats the full event search once per occurrence, and each single search may scan up to 800 days in 30-minute steps before refining. The visible-crescent path is even more expensive because each occurrence can perform up to 90 sunset searches. A malicious or careless staff-authored FutureProg, or a JuniorAdmin using the new time event preview command, can request a very large occurrence and consume CPU for a long period on the server thread. If that FutureProg is attached to hooks or commands reachable by normal players, players can repeatedly trigger the expensive search and cause an availability impact.

title: Herd driving bypasses exit access controls
description: The commit adds `field herd drive <herd> <direction> [count]`. The command resolves a visible exit and destination agriculture field, then immediately calls `DriveHerdTo`. Neither the command nor `DriveHerdTo` calls `actor.CanCross(exit)` or otherwise enforces normal exit blockers. In this codebase, normal character movement uses `CanCross` to prevent crossing closed doors, magical barriers, and guarded exits. Because herd driving only checks field/property ownership and pasture state, an authenticated player who can access a source field can move abstract herds through closed/locked doors, magical barriers, or guarded exits into an adjacent unowned/authorized field that the player could not physically enter. This is an authorization/integrity flaw in the game-world access-control boundary for valuable livestock state.

title: Craft field input bypasses field property authorization
description: This commit introduces AgricultureFieldInput for crafts. Its ScoutInput and IsInput methods accept any agriculture field in the actor's current location if the field state matches the craft requirements, and ReserveInput can immediately call ConsumeCropYield or ConsumeWoodlandYield. Unlike normal agriculture commands and field operations, this path does not check whether the field is on a property and whether the actor is an owner, leaseholder, or administrator. If a craft using this input is available, a normal authenticated player who can enter the room can use the craft system to consume another property's crop or woodland yield despite the agriculture command authorization checks denying direct field work.

title: Player field command can silently remove arbitrary NPCs
description: The agriculture module registers `field` as a normal player command and routes `field herd absorb` to code that targets any visible actor. The only field access check is property ownership/leaseholder status when the field is inside a property; unowned/public agriculture fields are allowed for everyone. Once a herd definition is selected, `AbsorbNpcIntoHerd` only rejects null targets, player characters, or NPCs outside the cell. It does not verify that the target NPC is an animal, livestock, controlled by the actor, tagged for agriculture, or created from the selected herd's NPC template. It then calls `npc.Quit(silent: true)` and increments the abstract herd count. A normal authenticated player standing in a field with an important NPC, such as a guard, merchant, quest mob, or admin-placed NPC, can remove that NPC from the live world by absorbing it into any herd definition.

title: Dragging suppresses linkdead logout indefinitely
description: This commit broadens no-timeout behavior from the new hitch use case to the generic Dragging effect. The disconnect handler checks CombinedEffectsOfType<INoTimeOutEffect>(); if any applies, it does not schedule the normal LinkdeadLogout and removes any existing one. Because the normal player 'drag' command creates a Dragging effect for any movable item and for eligible characters, a player can start dragging a holdable object and disconnect, leaving their character online indefinitely without the usual 10-minute cleanup. Similarly, a dragged character receives DragTarget, which now also implements INoTimeOutEffect, so an attacker who can drag an incapacitated or otherwise eligible player can prevent that player's linkdead timeout while the effect remains. This is an availability/resource-exhaustion issue and also affects account/session integrity for dragged victims.

title: Unhitch can cancel unrelated character drags
description: The commit adds `unhitch <character>` support for character and mount hitches, but the removal helper operates on all `Dragging` effects involving that character. Ordinary dragging is an existing movement/custody mechanic with its own checks, while character hitches are marked by a separate `CharacterHitch` effect. Because `Unhitch` calls `RemoveCharacterHitches` for any visible character and that helper removes unfiltered `Dragging` effects, any authenticated player in the same room can cancel another character's ordinary drag, including a guard/staff/NPC/player dragging a prisoner, unconscious character, or other target. This bypasses the normal authority/ownership/struggle controls for drag state and causes unauthorized in-game state manipulation.

title: Vehicle driving bypasses hidden cardinal exits
description: This commit redirects ordinary movement commands to vehicle movement for vehicle controllers and rewrites the drive command to use VehicleMovementCommand.TryMoveControlledVehicle. In that helper, if the normal string exit lookup fails, cardinal directions are resolved with vehicle.Location.GetExit(cardinal, actor). The cardinal exit lookup does not apply the normal exit visibility check. Normal walking movement separately checks Body.CanSee(Location, exit), and the old drive implementation only used the string lookup path, which filters invisible exits. As a result, a player controlling a vehicle can type or queue common directions such as north/east/etc. and traverse exits hidden from them by IExitHiddenEffect or equivalent visibility controls, if they can guess the direction.

title: Admin component enum input can terminate the server
description: ScribingImplement and InscribableSurface builder commands validate writing implement types with Enum.TryParse only. In .NET, Enum.TryParse accepts numeric strings even when the resulting value is not defined by the enum, so an Admin with component-editing access can run commands such as `comp set type 999` or `comp set implement add 999`. The code then stores or uses that invalid enum value and immediately calls Describe(), whose default case throws ApplicationException for unknown WritingImplementType values. PlayerConnection catches command exceptions by writing a crash log and calling Environment.Exit(0), so this becomes an authenticated staff-triggered denial of service. If invalid component definitions are otherwise loaded, later OLC display or item look/decorate paths can hit the same Describe() crash.

title: Manual combat commands allow ranged auxiliary attacks
description: The commit introduces data-driven manual combat commands and seeds a player-usable `bash` command bound to an auxiliary action that can knock down, delay, and drain stamina. The command handler permits using the current `CombatTarget` when no explicit target is supplied, then the resolver only verifies that the target is a member of the same combat. For auxiliary actions it calls `Race.UsableAuxiliaryMoves`, but auxiliary move usability checks intentions, attacker position, and a prog only; it does not enforce that the target is visible, in the same location/layer, or in melee range. `AuxiliaryMove.ResolveMove` then applies effects directly to the stored target. As a result, an authenticated player in a ranged/multi-room combat can invoke an auxiliary command such as `bash` against their current combat target from an invalid distance, bypassing normal physical targeting constraints and applying combat control effects remotely.

title: Poison can deliver on zero-severity wounds
description: This commit introduces weapon poison delivery with configurable multipliers. The defaults explicitly set WeaponPoisonSeverityMultiplierNone to 0.0, which appears intended to prevent poison delivery from wounds with WoundSeverity.None. However, CalculateDeliveryChance multiplies severity, damage, and wound-nature multipliers and then always applies Math.Clamp(..., minimum, maximum). With the default minimum of 0.05, a calculated chance of 0.0 caused by WoundSeverity.None is raised to 5%. DeliverOneCoating then treats that chance as eligible and can dose the target after the random roll. A remote authenticated player who can coat a weapon or ammunition and cause a zero/negligible severity wound object can therefore poison another character despite configuration saying that severity should not deliver. The calculation should return 0.0 when the severity multiplier or other gating multiplier is zero, and only apply the minimum clamp to positive base chances.

title: Execution patrol keeps stale condemned target after abort
description: ExecutionPatrolStrategy introduces mutable runtime fields such as _condemned, _stage, and _executionAttempts on the strategy object. Patrol objects do not receive a fresh strategy instance for ordinary route patrols; they keep a reference to route.PatrolStrategy. The strategy resets those fields only when its own CompleteExecution or AbortExecution paths run. However, Patrol.AbortPatrol can be reached outside ExecutionPatrolStrategy, for example when all patrol members are removed/dead and no leader remains, and that path deletes the patrol without calling ResetRuntimeState. Because the route strategy is then reused, EnsureCondemned immediately accepts a non-null stale _condemned and does not re-run SelectDueCondemned or check for a current AwaitingExecution effect. ReadyToBegin only proves that some due condemned character exists for the authority, not that the stale _condemned is still legally due. In a plausible abuse case, players can force an execution patrol to abort by killing/removing all enforcers, stale state remains, and a later patrol can apply no-quit, drag, restrain, or execute a character whose execution effect was subsequently removed or who is no longer the due target.

title: Imported mounts are universally rideable and controllable
description: The new BasicMount seed definition permits every character to mount and control any NPC that receives this AI, because both PermitRiderProg and PermitControlProg are set to the global AlwaysTrue FutureProg. The converter then automatically attaches BasicMount to ACT_MOUNT NPCs that resolve to supported mount races. The player-facing mount command relies on CanBeMountedBy, which delegates to the AI PermitRider check, and mount movement/control delegates to PermitControl. As a result, in deployments that seed this stock package and import RPI mount NPCs, any normal player can ride and command those imported mounts without an ownership, clan, stable-ticket, builder, or consent check. This can allow unauthorized relocation or commandeering of imported NPC mounts and related world-integrity abuse.
relevant_paths: DatabaseSeeder/Seeders/UsefulSeeder.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudNpcTransformer.cs | MudSharpCore/NPC/AI/MountAI.cs | MudSharpCore/Commands/Modules/RidingModule.cs

title: Stealth commands bypass planar inventory interaction checks
description: The commit adds player-accessible palm and steal inventory-transfer commands. These commands only require the attacker to see a target/container and be colocated in the same room/layer, then call Body.CanGet/CanPut and Body.Get/Put directly. They do not call the existing CanManipulateItem/CanInteractPlanar inventory checks that normal inventory commands use. ColocatedWith does not imply planar interaction, and CanSee may allow perception of entities on planes that cannot be physically affected. Because BodyInventory.CanGet/CanPut only checks container location access and container state, a visible open worn/held container whose Location is null can be manipulated even when the target or item is not interactable on the inventory plane. This lets a low-privileged player steal from or plant items into open visible containers/belts across intended planar/inventory isolation boundaries.

title: Unbounded virtual cash ledger queries can exhaust resources
description: The new VirtualCashLedger.LedgerEntries API takes an integer count and applies it directly to Take(count), then materializes the result with ToList(). Several newly added commands parse a user-supplied ledger count and only require it to be a positive int; there is no maximum cap. A player with access to these economy management surfaces can generate many ledger rows through repeated small deposit/withdraw/payment operations and then request a very large ledger count, forcing a large database read, allocation, sort/materialization, and text table generation on the game server. Because MUD command processing is availability-sensitive, this can degrade or stall service for other players.

title: Clan finance text fields can crash the server
description: The commit adds clan budget creation and drawdown commands. Both commands take free-form remaining command text via SafeRemainingArgument without validating or truncating it. Budget names are saved to ClanBudgets.Name, configured as varchar(200). Drawdown reasons are saved to ClanBudgetTransactions.Reason, configured as varchar(1000), and are also embedded into a bank account transaction description whose existing column is varchar(255). A player with CanCreateBudgets can create a budget with a name longer than 200 characters, causing SaveChanges to throw. A player who can draw a budget, including the holder/controller of the budgeted appointment, can submit an overlong reason and trigger the same class of exception. Player command exceptions are caught by PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand, logged, and then Environment.Exit(0) is called, so this becomes an authenticated remote denial of service against the MUD server.

title: Clairvoyance remote look bypasses visibility checks
description: ClairvoyancePower sends the player the output of RemoteLookRenderer.DescribeRemoteCell for the target's current location. The new renderer unconditionally sets PerceiveIgnoreFlags.IgnoreCanSee | PerceiveIgnoreFlags.IgnoreDark and then uses those flags when filtering and describing room items and characters. In this codebase, IgnoreCanSee causes CanSee to immediately return true, so this bypasses normal perception checks rather than merely producing a normal remote LOOK. A player who has this power and can select a target can therefore disclose hidden/invisible/dark-obscured room contents and occupants at that target's location, including information ordinary player perception should not reveal.

title: Unbounded forced wind movement can stall game loop
description: The commit introduces ForcedPathMovementEffect with a configurable Steps value. Steps is only constrained to be at least 1 when loaded from XML or set by the builder command, with no upper bound. GetOrApplyEffect then performs a synchronous for-loop from 0 to Steps, calling TryMoveTarget on each iteration and selecting the next exit in the same direction. On a cyclic set of exits, or on builder-created one-way directional loops, a malicious builder can configure Steps to a very large int value and cause a cast to monopolize the single game loop with repeated movement/teleport work. This is an availability issue because FutureMUD command and runtime processing is game-loop sensitive; the attack requires builder-level spell-editing privileges or an operator importing untrusted spell definitions.

title: Imported board name collisions bypass board access controls
description: RPI board import now derives a board key from the first source keyword, then builds the FutureMUD board name, component name, and access FutureProg name from that key. The board name is truncated to 45 characters, while component and FutureProg names are sanitized and truncated independently. The importer then reuses an existing generated component if the component name already exists, reuses an existing FutureProg if the generated function name already exists, and reuses a Board row solely by case-insensitive BoardName. There is no validation that a reused object was generated for the same legacy key or the same clan restrictions. A malicious or simply colliding worldfile can therefore cause two distinct legacy boards with different clan restrictions to share the same FutureMUD board/component/access program. Because board reads and writes authorize only through the board item component currently being used, but then list/read/write posts from the shared Board object, a player who can access the less-restricted imported board can read posts from, or post into, the shared backing board that should have been protected by another board's stricter clan access.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMUDItemTransformer.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudItemImporter.cs | MudSharpCore/Commands/Modules/CommunicationsModule.cs

title: Unchecked ordinal interval parsing can kill the server
description: This commit adds ordinal monthly interval forms such as "every month on day 15" and wires them into the player-facing property interval command. The new regexes accept arbitrary-length digit strings, but TryParseOrdinalDay and TryParseOrdinalWeekday convert those captures with int.Parse without catching OverflowException. For example, an authenticated player who owns a property with a lease order and is at a conveyancing location can submit a property interval like "every month on day 999999999999999999999999999999". RecurringInterval.TryParse is expected to return false for invalid input, but instead the exception unwinds through PropertyInterval. In release builds PlayerConnection catches any command exception, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), causing a server-wide denial of service.

title: Forced movement can target nonparticipants in friendly combat
description: The commit adds player-accessible `shove` and `pull` commands for forced movement. In friendly combat, the command resolves the target with `actor.TargetActor(targetText)`, which can return any visible character in the room, not just a sparring participant. The code does not check that the selected target is in `actor.Combat`, is targeting or targeted by the actor, or has consented to the friendly bout. The subsequent range check only requires the attacker to have the global `MeleeRange` flag, not that the attacker is in melee with the selected target. If the forced movement succeeds, the target is moved with `Teleport` through an exit or to a room layer. A player in a spar can therefore attempt to shove or pull a bystander into water, air, rooftops, or through exits, potentially bypassing the consent boundary of friendly combat and causing drowning/fall/environmental harm.

title: Unbounded MudDateTime weekday search enables CPU DoS
description: WeekdayFunction.GetOccurrenceCount only requires the optional Occurrences argument to be an integral decimal within the int range. The MudDateTime implementation then calls GetMudWeekday, which loops while occurrences > 0 and calls result.Date.AdvanceDays(direction) one day at a time. On a normal seven-day calendar, NextWeekday(date, "Monday", 2147483647) requires roughly 15 billion loop iterations. This bypasses the explicit FutureProg while-loop iteration cap because the work happens inside a built-in function. The function is registered without restricting compilation contexts, and the computer-program restrictions allow Number, Text, and MudDateTime and do not block the DateTime category, so the new helper is available to player-facing computer programs as well as standard FutureProgs. Executing such a program is synchronous, so a malicious authenticated player with programming access, or a builder who can author FutureProgs, can monopolize the game thread/CPU and cause a denial of service.

title: Unprivileged stable info triggers global fee assessment
description: The new `stable` player command dispatches the empty/info subcommand to `StableInfo`, which only checks that the actor is currently at a stable and then calls `stable.Show(actor)`. `Stable.Show` is not a read-only public view: it calls `AssessAllActiveStays()` before rendering, and that loops over every active stay. Each assessment can load the stabled mount and original owner, execute the configured daily-fee FutureProg, and create ledger entries/database writes when a day has elapsed. Because the command has no stable manager/proprietor check, any logged-in player standing at a stable can repeatedly trigger this workload on the main game loop. The same output also reveals internal details such as the stable bank account reference, FutureProg identifiers, employee list, and stable account counts to non-staff/non-employees.

title: Negative hotel room charges allow bank draining
description: The hotel room configuration paths parse currency amounts but never require them to be non-negative or positive. Default currency parsing supports signed amounts, so an authorised hotel/property manager can set a room to a large negative daily price and a positive security deposit. When a patron rents the room, the code computes total = rentalCharge + securityDeposit + taxCharge. If the negative rental charge makes total <= 0, TryPayHotel returns success without withdrawing any cash or depositing anything into the hotel bank. RentHotelRoom nevertheless records the positive security deposit on the rental. On checkout, CompleteHotelStay credits that security deposit as a patron balance, and TryPayoutHotelBalance withdraws the balance from the hotel bank and creates cash for the patron. This can be used by a malicious property/clan hotel manager and an accomplice account to drain a configured hotel bank account or corrupt the in-game economy.

title: Persistent zero-g tethers can crash on deleted anchors
description: The commit introduces the `tether` command and the persistent `ZeroGravityTether` effect. A player can create a tether to any locally targetable perceivable anchor. The effect saves only the anchor type/id and is marked as saving, but it does not subscribe to anchor deletion, validate that the anchor is permanent, or handle `Gameworld.GetPerceivable` returning null on load. If the anchor is a normal item that is later deleted/junked/decays and the tether effect is saved, then after a restart the constructor can set `Anchor` to null. Subsequent `SaveDefinition`, `Describe`, or `BlocksMovementTo` calls dereference `Anchor` unconditionally, which can throw `NullReferenceException`. In the MUD server context, an authenticated player with access to a zero-gravity tether item and a deletable anchor can create a persistent malformed effect that can crash command processing, autosave, or potentially the game loop when the affected character is loaded or moved in zero gravity.

title: Cook list empty filter can terminate the server
description: This commit adds the player-accessible `cook` command and its `CookList` filter parser. `StringStack.PopSpeech()` can return an empty string for a quoted empty argument (`""`). `CookList` loops while the stack is not finished, assigns that empty string to `arg`, and immediately evaluates `arg[0]`. That throws `IndexOutOfRangeException`. In release builds, `PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand` catches any exception during player input, writes a crash log, and calls `Environment.Exit(0)`, so any authenticated player able to issue character commands can shut down the MUD process. The same unchecked pattern appears to have existed in the older `crafts` list command, but this commit introduces the vulnerable parser into the new `cook list` surface; both should be fixed by ignoring/rejecting empty arguments before indexing.

title: Admin-controlled plane room format can DoS the server
description: The new RoomNameFormat feature accepts any string that merely contains the literal substring "{0}" and then passes it directly to string.Format. Composite format alignment components such as repeated "{0,9999999}" entries can expand a few hundred bytes of input into hundreds of megabytes of output. The setter itself renders an example with string.Format, and if the format is saved, every room name render for characters on that plane repeats the allocation. This can stall or exhaust the single game loop and may trigger the existing player-input crash path that exits the process. The affected command is gated to PermissionLevel.Admin, so this is not unauthenticated, but it gives a mid-tier staff account a shutdown/availability impact normally reserved for higher staff.

title: Unbounded race satiation limits can crash server
description: The commit adds configurable racial satiation limits, but the new builder validation only requires the value to parse as a double and be greater than zero. Finite subnormal values such as 5E-324 are accepted. Runtime sanitization also accepts any finite positive value. In FulfilNeeds, FoodSatiatedHours is increased and then, before NormaliseValues clamps it, the alcohol absorption delay divides by GetFoodAbsolutelyStuffedThreshold(FoodSatiationLimit), i.e. 75% of the configured food limit. With a subnormal/tiny limit and an alcoholic liquid mixture that has any positive food satiation, this division can produce Infinity and TimeSpan.FromMinutes throws OverflowException. In release builds, PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand catches command exceptions and calls Environment.Exit(0), so the triggering drink command can shut down the MUD process. This requires an Admin-level staff account to set the malicious race limit, but the resulting impact is process-wide availability loss and can then be triggered through normal gameplay.

title: Stock keypads use predictable default unlock codes
description: The commit adds seeded modern security/automation components. The new stock keypad prototypes are created with fixed codes "1234" and "246810". The same seed pass creates an ElectronicLock_Maglock whose signal source is the 4-digit keypad prototype. If an operator installs this package and builders use these stock components without changing the code, an authenticated player can guess or know the default code and activate the keypad signal to unlock the electronic lock. This does not compromise server accounts or host resources, but it can bypass in-game access controls and affect world integrity/confidentiality for areas or containers protected by these components.

title: Expired transform spells leave characters in forced forms
description: SpellTransformFormEffect.RemovalEffect now delegates reversion to ReevaluateForcedBodyTransformation. However, EffectHandler.RemoveEffect invokes RemovalEffect before removing the effect from the owner's effect list. During that reevaluation, GetActiveForcedTransformationDemands enumerates all SpellTransformFormEffect instances on the character and does not exclude the effect currently being removed. As a result, the resolver sees the expiring transform spell as an active demand, returns early, and does not remove the ForcedTransformationBaselineEffect or restore the saved baseline body. A player who can trigger a temporary transform spell can remain in the spell-granted body after the spell duration ends, at least until a later reevaluation such as relogin. If alternate forms grant stronger body traits, race capabilities, access, or combat advantages, this is an integrity bypass of spell duration enforcement.

title: Unsanitized transformation echoes can crash game loop
description: Custom transformation echo text is accepted verbatim from admin form editing, magic spell effect editing, merit editing, and the new FutureProg mutator. When a character switches forms, the echo is wrapped in an internal Emote and broadcast. Emote.ParseFor ultimately calls string.Format(RawText, parsedTokens), so unescaped '{' or '}' characters, or out-of-range placeholders such as '{999}', can raise a FormatException. The main game loop does not contain per-command exception isolation around ProcessPendingCommands, so this can become a persistent availability issue once a bad echo is saved. Existing player-facing emotes use Sanitise() or related escaping for this reason; these new echo setters do not.

title: Hard-coded database credentials in new batch scripts
description: Two newly committed batch files embed the database account `futuremud` and password `rpiengine2020` directly in source-controlled files. `Backup-MUD.bat` uses them to run `mysqldump`, and `Start-MUD.bat` passes the same password in the MudSharp command-line connection string. If these scripts are copied into a deployment, published with the repository, or left readable on a host, anyone with access to the files can recover the database credentials. If the database is reachable from the attacker, those credentials can allow full read/write access to account, character, world, staff, and configuration data. Passing the password on the command line also risks exposing it to local process-list inspection while the server starts.

title: Craft import can persist crashing emote format strings
description: Craft phase echo and failure text from crafts.txt is treated as trusted runtime emote text. The parser reads directives such as 1st/3rd/1stfail via ReadExtendedText, the transformer assigns those raw strings to ConvertedCraftPhaseDefinition.Echo and FailEcho, and the importer stores them as CraftPhase.Echo/FailEcho. FutureMUD emotes ultimately call string.Format(RawText, parsedTokens), and the Emote constructor documents that raw emote strings must not contain '{' characters. A malicious legacy craft file containing an unmatched brace or invalid format item in an imported phase echo can therefore persist a craft that throws FormatException when a player reaches that craft phase. Because craft phases run from the main effect/game loop, this can become a persistent availability issue after an operator imports an untrusted worldfile.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/RpiCraftParser.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudCraftTransformer.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudCraftImporter.cs | MudSharpCore/Work/Crafts/Craft.cs | MudSharpCore/PerceptionEngine/Parsers/Emote.cs

title: Unbounded sever formulas can halt the game loop
description: The new `severformula` bodypart builder command accepts any NCalc expression as long as `HasErrors()` is false. That check only validates parse syntax; it does not evaluate the expression, validate function arity, restrict expensive functions, or impose a length/complexity/runtime budget. The new damage path then constructs and evaluates the stored formula inside `ShouldSever` without a local try/catch or timeout. Existing ExpressionEngine custom functions can throw ordinary `ArgumentException` for bad arity such as `rand(1)` or can do attacker-selected linear work such as `dice(1000000000,6)`. Because damage processing runs from the main command/game loop, a SeniorAdmin who can edit bodyparts can persist such a formula and any later combat hit to the affected bodypart can crash or stall service for all connected players.

title: Boards app reads posts without network authentication
description: The commit introduces a network-facing Boards built-in application. Its command dispatcher passes the authenticated account only to post/delete paths, while list and read are handled without requiring or validating an account. HandleList and HandleRead only require that a board host and board are selected, then call ComputerBoardService.GetPosts/ReadPost. Those service methods only verify that the board is hosted, not that the caller is authenticated or allowed to access the content. As a result, any player who can reach an advertised Boards host through the in-game network can enumerate post metadata and read full board contents without possessing valid credentials for the host's shared identity domain. This conflicts with the documented expected behavior that Boards authenticate via shared user@domain identities for reading and creation/deletion.

title: VPN tunnel grants any domain account all gateway routes
description: The commit introduces session VPN tunnelling, but the authorization check is too coarse. After resolving a reachable gateway, TryOpenTunnel authenticates the supplied user@domain and only checks whether the authenticated account's domain is hosted by the gateway. It does not check whether that account, user, or domain is authorized for the requested VPN network. The selected VPN network is then chosen solely from gateway.Host.HostedVpnNetworkIds and the corresponding route key is added to the terminal session. As a result, any valid account on any enabled domain hosted by a gateway can request any VPN network exposed by that gateway, including private routes that should be limited to other users or roles. Because the same network accounts are shared with mail/identity functionality, a low-privilege mailbox account can become sufficient to discover and access VPN-only simulated hosts and services.

title: Network switches can recursively crash server
description: NetworkSwitchGameItemComponent accepts any INetworkInfrastructure, including another NetworkSwitchGameItemComponent, as a valid connection target. A normal connection is recorded on both ends. When the second side of a switch-to-switch connection is recorded, RawConnect calls NotifyConnectedAdaptersTopologyChanged, which recursively calls the same method on every connected switch with no visited set or cycle guard. Even a simple A<->B switch connection therefore recurses B -> A -> B indefinitely, causing a StackOverflowException or equivalent process crash. The public player 'connect' command can reach this logic for manipulable in-game items, so an authenticated player with access to two switch items can cause a denial of service.

title: Unbounded file-signal writes can exhaust server resources
description: This commit adds FileSignalGenerator, a component with a configurable FileCapacityInBytes and a mutable ComputerMutableFileSystem. The component exposes its file system through IComputerFileOwner, and ComputerFileTransferUtilities now publishes component-owned file systems to FileManager/FTP when they are on a computer host. However, ComputerMutableFileSystem.WriteFile and AppendFile store attacker-controlled text without checking CapacityInBytes or any other size limit. Every write to the configured signal file then raises FileChanged and the signal generator immediately trims and parses the entire file as a double. A player who can access the new programming file command, or a user with FTP/FileManager write access to the host-local file owner, can repeatedly write or append large contents. This can grow persistent XML/DB state without bound and force synchronous full-file parsing/allocation on the game thread, causing resource exhaustion and possible service denial. The underlying file-system capacity enforcement weakness existed before for host/storage files, but this commit introduces the new file-signal surface and change-triggered parsing path.

title: FTP login allows account enumeration and brute force
description: The commit introduces an in-game FTP authentication flow. Failed logins return to the input loop immediately, with no counter, delay, lockout, or source/account throttling in the login path. The authenticator also emits distinct error messages for an unknown or disabled FTP username versus a valid username with a bad password. A normal player with access to a terminal and a reachable advertised FTP host can therefore enumerate FTP account names and perform online password guessing. If a password is guessed, the same service grants authenticated read/write/delete access to files on the target host and mounted storage devices.

title: Oversized mail subjects can terminate the server
description: The commit introduces the Mail built-in application and mail persistence tables. A logged-in mailbox user can set an arbitrary subject with the terminal command `type subject <text>`. The application only checks that the subject is non-empty before calling `SendMessage`, which assigns `subject.Trim()` to `ComputerMailMessage.Subject` and immediately calls `SaveChanges()`. The EF model maps `Subject` to `varchar(500)`, but no application-side maximum length is enforced. On a normal MySQL deployment with strict length enforcement, a subject longer than 500 characters will cause a database exception. Player command exceptions are caught by `PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand`, which writes a crash log and calls `Environment.Exit(0)`, so this becomes an authenticated denial of service for the whole MUD process.

title: FileManager allows unbounded persistent file writes
description: This commit makes FileManager a shipped built-in application and adds player-driven file mutation commands. Those commands call WriteFile, AppendFile, and copy/edit save paths directly, but neither FileManager nor ComputerMutableFileSystem checks whether the resulting file contents would exceed CapacityInBytes. The multiline editor path is especially risky because the editor accumulates arbitrary lines and then saves the full text to the computer file system. Since computer item state is persisted as XML/CDATA, a low-privileged authenticated player with access to a powered terminal can create or append large persistent files beyond the configured storage capacity, causing persistent memory/database/disk growth and expensive output when files are shown or listed.

title: Built-in apps persist undeletable process records
description: This commit adds player-facing built-in application execution through `programming app <name>`. Each invocation calls `ExecuteBuiltInApplication`, which creates a host process before invoking the built-in executor. SysMon then returns `Completed`, and unimplemented built-ins return `Failed`, but `ApplyExecutionOutcome_NoLock` persists both terminal states. For real host components, `SaveProcessDefinition` keeps the process in `_processes`, and the host serializes all processes to item XML. The normal `kill` path explicitly refuses completed, failed, or killed processes, and built-in applications are not normal stored executables that a player can delete to remove their historical process rows. As a result, any player able to connect to a powered terminal can repeatedly run `programming app sysmon` or reserved unimplemented apps and permanently bloat the host's in-memory and persisted process list, increasing save/load size and causing increasingly expensive process listings and SysMon output.

title: WaitSignal can crash on non-finite signal values
description: This commit introduces WaitSignal(), which resumes a suspended computer program when a signal source emits a non-zero double value. The resume handler treats any value that is not close to zero as resumable, but it does not reject NaN or Infinity. When the resumed WaitSignalFunction consumes the pending signal, it calls Convert.ToDecimal(pendingSignal.Value.Value). Convert.ToDecimal(double.NaN) and Convert.ToDecimal(double.PositiveInfinity/NegativeInfinity) throw OverflowException. ComputerProgramExecutor only catches ComputerProgramWaitException, and the main game loop has a try/finally without a catch, so this exception can propagate out of command/scheduler processing and halt the server. Existing signal source builder commands accept values through double.TryParse without checking double.IsFinite, so non-finite values can be introduced into signal components and then triggered while a WaitSignal program is waiting.

title: Unbounded global process scan on terminal input
description: This commit replaced the previous owner-scoped lookup of waiting terminal-input processes with a global search over all workspace processes plus every computer host/storage process found by enumerating all gameworld items. The search is performed inside the ComputerExecutionService global lock on every `type` command before the code even knows whether anything is waiting for input. A low-privileged player near a usable terminal can repeatedly issue `type ...` and force O(world items + processes) work under the shared lock, which can block other computer execution and, in a large MUD, consume main-loop/server time. The duplicate-wait check on program suspension also uses the same global scan. The lookup should be indexed by waiting character and terminal, or at least constrained to the connected terminal/host/current owner rather than enumerating all items.

title: Recursive launchprogram can crash the game loop
description: The new `LaunchProgramFunction` is registered for computer programs and resolves the target program by attacker-controlled text from the current owner. It then immediately calls `ComputerExecutionService.Execute` for that program. `ComputerExecutionService.Execute` creates a process and calls `ComputerProgramExecutor.Execute` synchronously, so a program can re-enter the same execution path before the prior invocation completes. There is no check for launching the currently executing program, no ancestor/depth limit, no per-owner process quota, and no async scheduling boundary. An authenticated player with the player-facing `programming` command enabled can create a private workspace program such as one named `loop` whose source calls `launchprogram("loop")`, then execute it once. This can recurse until stack exhaustion, CPU exhaustion, or large numbers of persisted workspace processes are created, impacting availability of the MUD server/game loop.

title: Cyclic automation mounts can recurse item lifecycle to DoS
description: AutomationMountHostGameItemComponent now calls Login(), Quit(), and Delete() on every mounted item's parent. Those GameItem lifecycle methods in turn dispatch to every component, including any AutomationMountHostGameItemComponent on the same or a cyclically mounted item. However, CanInstallModule/InstallModule only checks bay compatibility and whether the module is already mounted; it does not reject module.Parent == Parent or reject installing an ancestor/descendant item. The player-facing electrical install command resolves host and module items independently, so a hybrid item with both an automation host and an automation mountable microcontroller, or two such hybrid items, can be arranged into a self/cyclic mount. Once that item logs in, quits, or is deleted, the new forwarding recursively calls item.Login()/Quit()/Delete() until StackOverflowException, which is process-fatal in .NET and can deny service to the MUD.

title: Keypad ships with valid default code and no lockout
description: The introduced Keypad component initializes every new keypad prototype with Code = "1234" and CanSubmit only verifies that the code is non-empty and numeric, so the known default code is production-valid unless a builder remembers to change it. At runtime, Select compares each supplied numeric argument directly to the stored code and returns after failure without recording attempts, delaying retries, locking the keypad, or otherwise rate-limiting guesses. Because keypads are intended to drive electronic doors, locks, relays, and similar automation, a remote authenticated player who can interact with a deployed keypad can try the known default first and then brute-force numeric codes online to activate protected in-game systems.

title: Electrical diagnostics leak hidden cable routes
description: The new ShowElectricalStatus path builds nearbyCables from EnumerateNearbySignalItems and then prints each cable's parent item, routed source binding, route direction, live/broken status, current signal value, and endpoint. EnumerateNearbySignalItems adds every item from cell.LayerGameItems(anchorItem.RoomLayer) and attached/connected items, but does not filter those items with actor.CanSee, CanManipulateItem, or another access check before returning them. Because electrical is a PlayerCommand, any authenticated player who can manipulate a visible signal-capable electrical item in the room can cause the status output to include otherwise hidden or non-visible SignalCableSegmentGameItemComponent items in the same room/layer. This can reveal concealed security/alarm wiring topology and live sensor state, aiding bypass of in-game automation security. Housing contents are still gated by CanAccessHousing, but hidden/obscured standalone or attached cable items in the room are not.

title: Unbounded electronic door signal reconnect heartbeat
description: ElectronicDoorGameItemComponent.ReconnectSource now subscribes the door to the global SecondHeartbeat whenever its configured upstream signal source is missing. HeartbeatTick then calls ReconnectSource again every second while the source remains missing. There is no retry counter, timeout, backoff, or distinction between a transient load-order race and a permanently invalid or removed binding. Each retry calls LocalSignalSinkSubscription.Reconnect, which ultimately searches accessible signal items in the room/attached item graph. A player or builder who can cause many doors to have unresolved signal bindings could force persistent per-second work on the main heartbeat/game loop, creating an avoidable availability risk. The nearby PoweredMachineBaseGameItemComponent retry added by the same commit is bounded to five attempts, which highlights the missing bound on the door retry path.

title: Generic connect bypasses automation bay access checks
description: This commit adds automation mount hosts and mountable microcontrollers as IConnectable items. The intended installation path is the electrical install command, which runs a delayed electrical action and ultimately calls InstallModule, whose CanInstallModule check rejects installation when CanAccessMounts fails, such as when a required maintenance panel is closed. However, because the new components also expose generic IConnectable behavior, a player can use the existing connect <module> <host> command. That command only checks normal item manipulation and then calls CanConnect/Connect. The new AutomationMountHostGameItemComponent.CanConnect and MicrocontrollerGameItemComponent.CanConnect/Connect methods do not call CanAccessMounts or CanInstallModule, so they permit immediate installation into a sealed/locked maintenance bay and bypass the tool, skill, delay, and shock-risk workflow. This undermines the access-control boundary introduced for concealed automation hardware and can allow unauthorized tampering with in-game automation such as locks, doors, alarms, or controllers.

title: Timer sensor duration parsing lets admins crash the server
description: TimerSensorGameItemComponentProto validates active and inactive duration input with only double.TryParse and value <= 0.0. .NET accepts inputs such as "NaN", "Infinity", and very large exponents like "1e10000" as doubles; these pass the positive-number check but TimeSpan.FromSeconds throws ArgumentException or OverflowException. The component command is available to PermissionLevel.Admin, and player-input exceptions are caught in PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand by writing a crash log and calling Environment.Exit(0). Therefore, an authenticated Admin-level account can issue a timer sensor duration command with a non-finite/oversized value and reliably shut down the server.

title: Microcontroller signal cycles can crash the game loop
description: The commit introduces same-item signal sources and microcontrollers. Microcontroller inputs accept arbitrary sibling signal source component names, and the runtime resolver only excludes the component itself. This means two or more microcontrollers can be wired in a feedback cycle, e.g. A reads B and B reads A. When a source changes, the handler recomputes immediately and SetOutput synchronously invokes SignalChanged. If the controller logic changes the value each pass, such as returning input + 1, the event chain recurses indefinitely until stack exhaustion, numeric overflow, or main-loop starvation. Because this runs in the MUD server's command/load path, a lower-privileged builder or malicious imported content can create an item that causes an availability failure when powered/loaded or when any player operates the item.

title: Legacy scar applications can crash the server
description: Scar template rows are now deliberately converted to null and skipped during world loading. However, existing chargen XML can still contain legacy SelectedDisfigurements entries that point at those scar template IDs. Chargen.LoadFromXml unconditionally adds the lookup result to SelectedDisfigurements, even when the template lookup returns null. LoadFromXml then calls RecalculateCurrentCosts, which reaches DisfigurementPickerScreenStoryboard.ChargenCosts and dereferences disfigurement.Disfigurement.ChargenCosts. For a legacy scar selection this is a NullReferenceException. In release builds, PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand catches any player-input exception, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), so an authenticated player resuming a pre-upgrade in-progress chargen with a scar selection can terminate the server. Submitted legacy applications may similarly crash staff/review paths that load or recalculate costs. The migration/refactor should either migrate old scar selections into SelectedScars or ignore/null-check legacy scar disfigurements when loading and when calculating costs.

title: Tiered laws now let criminals outrank citizens
description: Before this change, tier comparisons were generated from a fixed social order where Citizen and Non-Citizen outranked Felon and Criminal. The commit now generates tier checks using LegalClassOutranks, which compares LegalClassPriority numerically. The seeded priorities place Enforcer=60, Felon=55, Criminal=54, Citizen=50, and Non-Citizen=40. As a result, when a character resolves to the Criminal or Felon legal class, LegalClassOutranks reports that they outrank ordinary citizens/non-citizens. For tiered victim-based crimes, the generated 'Against Inferior' law variant is selected when the offender outranks the victim, and that variant disables active enforcement, imprisonment, capital punishment, arrest, and bail handling, leaving only a fine. A player who becomes wanted/criminal or is a felon can therefore receive weaker automatic enforcement for later serious crimes against citizens in affected seeded legal systems.

title: Arena scoring snapshots leak memory across completed events
description: The new scoring path records an ArenaScoringSnapshot for every scorable combat resolution between participants in any live arena event. These snapshots include attacker/defender character references and impact strings, are exposed through ScoringSnapshots, and are copied into multiple lists whenever event prog arguments are built. The only clear happens in StartLive before recording begins. FinalizeParticipants does not clear the list, and CombatArena keeps created and loaded events in its _events list even after they are completed or aborted, merely filtering them out of ActiveEvents. As a result, every completed arena event can retain all combat-resolution snapshots for the life of the process. An authenticated player who can participate in scheduled/recurring arena fights can increase memory use over time through normal combat activity, potentially causing server availability degradation or eventual memory exhaustion on long-running servers. The fix should clear _scoringSnapshots during cleanup/completion/abort/finalization, consider recording only for event types that actually need scoring snapshots, and/or cap or aggregate scoring data instead of storing unbounded per-hit records.

title: Tattoo copy can exfiltrate arbitrary writing text
description: The commit adds `slot=copy:writingId` support to the player-accessible `tattoo inscribe` command. That parser resolves the supplied ID directly through `actor.Gameworld.Writings.Get(writingId)`, extracts the underlying `SimpleWriting.Text` or `CompositeWriting.Text`, and stores it in the tattoo text value. There is no authorization check tying the writing to an item the actor can see/hold/target, and `actor.CanRead(writing)` is only used to add a skill penalty, not to prevent copying or reading the raw text. A player with tattooing capability and a consenting/controlled target can therefore enumerate or guess writing IDs and copy private letters, books, graffiti, or other writings into a tattoo. The copied text is later rendered into writing markup. Additionally, the new `ITattoo.ShortDescription` implementation renders via `ShortDescriptionFor(null)`, and `SubstituteWrittenLanguage` returns the readable text when the perceiver is null; remaining call sites such as the tattoo completion emote call `Tattoo.ShortDescription.SubstituteWrittenLanguage(...)`, which can expose tattoo text without the intended language/script checks.

title: Unescaped snapshot database name permits SQL injection
description: The commit adds a fast path that imports DatabaseSeeder/BlankDatabaseSnapshot.sql for databases that appear blank. That SQL file places the __FUTUREMUD_DATABASE__ placeholder inside backtick-quoted MySQL identifiers. DatabaseUpgradeCoordinator.ImportBlankDatabaseSnapshot replaces the placeholder with MySqlConnectionStringBuilder.Database verbatim and then executes the whole script with MySqlScript. A crafted database name containing a backtick and additional SQL can break out of the quoted identifier and execute arbitrary SQL as the configured MySQL user during seeder bootstrap. This is not reachable from normal MUD clients, but it is security-relevant for deployment tooling or hosted/automated environments where a lower-trust party can influence the seeder connection string or database name.

title: Unbounded fax queue enables persistent DoS
description: The commit introduces a player-accessible fax command and a receiver-side pending fax queue. A sender can repeatedly fax the same readable document to a powered destination fax machine that has no paper/ink or cannot fit the content. The destination still satisfies CanReceiveFaxes, TrySendFax delivers the document, and ReceiveFax unconditionally appends a new IncomingFaxJob to _pendingFaxes. Jobs are only removed after successfully printing; if supplies remain unavailable or a document cannot fit on blank paper, the queue remains and future faxes continue to accumulate. The full pending queue is serialized into the game item XML, so abuse can grow live memory, save data, load-time work, and later printing work without a bounded in-game resource cost to the sender. Shared fax numbers can also multiply delivery to multiple receivers.

title: Hosted voicemail allows unbounded persistent message storage
description: When a call rings out to hosted voicemail, every transmitted speech segment from the caller is appended to an in-memory list and later stored in the grid's central voicemail dictionary. The storage path simply appends to the mailbox and serializes all stored recordings into the grid XML. Unlike the existing physical answering-machine path, there is no tape capacity check or any other per-message/per-mailbox cap. A normal authenticated player who can place calls to a voicemail-enabled line can repeatedly leave long messages and cause unbounded memory growth during calls and unbounded persistent database/XML growth after saves, potentially exhausting disk/database storage or making grid load/save increasingly expensive.

title: Unbounded answering-machine greeting recording DoS
description: A player who can manipulate an answering machine can start greeting recording and then keep speaking near the machine. Each speech event appends a RecordedAudioSegment, including raw text, to _workingGreetingSegments. Unlike message recording, greeting recording does not check Tape.CanStoreRecording or any segment/duration limit before appending. The tape capacity check happens only later when the user issues greeting stop, so an attacker can simply never stop recording and grow server memory until the process becomes unstable or dies.

title: Telecom feeder power loops can crash the server
description: TelecommunicationsGridFeederGameItemComponent implements IProducePower by delegating MaximumPowerInWatts and ProducingPower to its _connectedPowerSource. This commit changed Parent_OnConnected to accept a connected IProducePower when either PrimaryExternalConnectionPowerProducer is true or MaximumPowerInWatts is currently positive. Because another telecom feeder can have MaximumPowerInWatts > 0 by delegating to its own upstream source, two already-powered feeders can be connected together and each can overwrite its upstream source with the other feeder. That creates A._connectedPowerSource = B and B._connectedPowerSource = A. Subsequent reads of MaximumPowerInWatts, ProducingPower, PowerConsumptionInWatts, or disconnect handling recurse until StackOverflowException, which is typically process-terminating in .NET. The normal player 'connect' command reaches this code for manipulable compatible items, so an authenticated player with access to appropriate powered feeder objects can cause a server availability failure.

title: Implant phones bypass neural control checks
description: Implant telephones are intended to be controlled through a neural-interface command surface, but the component also implements the generic ITelephone/ITransmit interfaces. The existing player-facing dial/answer/pickup/hangup/transmitwith commands target any item exposing ITelephone/ITransmit and only require normal item manipulation. The new ImplantTelephoneGameItemComponent's Dial/CanDial and Transmit methods check grid, power, coverage, and call state, but do not verify HasAudioAccess, a powered audio-capable DNI link, or that the actor is the installed body's actor issuing the command through IImplantNeuralLink. As a result, a player can use standard room/inventory phone commands on a targetable external implant telephone, bypassing the intended neural-interface restrictions. The same missing boundary also makes the implant's IReceive implementation accept speech from arbitrary transmit origins and deliver it internally whenever HasAudioAccess is true, without checking that the speech belongs to the current telephone call.

title: Cell phone signal checks allow item-scan DoS
description: The commit introduces cellular phones and towers. CellularPhoneGameItemComponent.HasCoverage calls HasCoverageInZone, which enumerates Gameworld.Items and inspects every item for an ICellPhoneTower. HasCoverage is used in full item descriptions, dialing, answering, and CanReceiveCalls. The player-facing dial command invokes telephone.Dial, and incoming calls also evaluate CanReceiveCalls for target phones. In a large MUD world, an authenticated player can repeatedly trigger O(total loaded items) scans on the main game loop by looking at a cellular phone, dialing from one, or dialing a cellular phone number. Because MUD command processing is latency-sensitive and often single-loop driven, this can be used to degrade availability. Coverage should be indexed or cached by grid/zone/tower membership rather than discovered by scanning every game item on each command.

title: Grid liquid source merge can crash the server
description: GridLiquidSourceGameItemComponent is still exposed as an ILiquidContainer, but its MergeLiquid implementation now unconditionally throws. Normal fill/pour commands mostly avoid this because the same commit sets LiquidCapacity to 0.0 and those commands check capacity first. However, generic liquid-application paths can still identify the grid source as an ILiquidContainer and call MergeLiquid directly. CreateLiquidEffect is compatible with item-target spell triggers and, for item targets with an ILiquidContainer component, calls container.MergeLiquid when CanMerge is true. LiquidMixture.CanMerge currently always returns true. If a normal player can cast an item-target create-liquid spell at a visible grid liquid source item, this exception propagates out of command processing. PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand catches it, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), turning the invalid operation into a process-wide denial of service.

title: Grid liquid sources do not consume liquid on Reduce operations
description: The commit adds GridLiquidSourceGameItemComponent as an ILiquidContainer backed by a liquid grid. Its LiquidMixture and LiquidVolume report the grid contents, but ReduceLiquidQuantity, AddLiquidQuantity, and MergeLiquid are empty methods. Existing code assumes ILiquidContainer.ReduceLiquidQuantity actually removes liquid after actions such as drinking or emptying a container. As a result, an authenticated player who can access a grid liquid source can repeatedly drink from it without depleting the underlying suppliers, and if the source can be carried or otherwise used with empty/craft paths, can create puddles or products from the same grid liquid repeatedly. This breaks world/economy integrity and can also be abused for availability by generating many liquid/puddle objects.

title: Estate-less morgue corpses can bypass item ownership
description: Previously morgue intake always ensured an estate existed and stripped corpse possessions into a morgue belongings bundle. The claim path for items from that bundle only releases items whose Owner is the claiming actor. This commit allows EnsureEstate to return null when estates are disabled or when CreateEstatesForCharacterDeath creates no estate. In that no-estate case, IntakeCorpse still stores the corpse but skips stripping belongings. The new corpse-claim authorization then allows the deceased character's EstateHeir to claim an estate-less corpse, and MorgueClaimCorpse releases the entire corpse into the claimant's room. Because estate creation explicitly ignores items that have an owner other than the deceased, a corpse containing only other players' owned items can be stored with EstateId 0 and all belongings still attached. The deceased's heir can then retrieve the corpse and potentially access/loot those items, bypassing the intended owner-only MorgueClaimItem path.

title: Strip acceptance grants broad inventory consent
description: The commit changes accepted strip requests to add a temporary BeDressedEffect to the target for the computed strip duration plus five seconds. BeDressedEffect is a general consent effect used by WillingToPermitInventoryManipulation and CanBeRemoved/CanDress, not a strip-specific authorization. During that window, the requester can perform other inventory actions against the victim, such as taking held, wielded, or other direct items, or interacting with permitted containers, even though the victim only accepted a specific strip request. This is especially problematic because the Take command is instant and relies on CanBeRemoved/WillingToPermitInventoryManipulation; DirectItems includes wielded, held, and worn items. If the target has no worn items, the strip duration can be zero while still granting five seconds of broad consent.

title: Admin prog result display can crash the game loop
