description: The updated Preview command now calls GetItemType<IContainer>() on a single item being previewed and unconditionally lists every item in container.Contents. This bypasses the normal container description visibility rules, which only decorate Contents/Evaluate output when a container is open or transparent. As a result, a normal player who can access a shop can preview a closed, locked, or opaque container offered for sale and learn the short descriptions and count of items inside without opening or buying it. The impact is limited to in-game confidentiality, but it is an introduced information disclosure in a player-facing command.

title: Unsafe craft CSV export enables formula injection
description: The commit adds `impdebug exportcrafts`, which serializes craft names, categories, blurbs, action descriptions, phase echoes, inputs, tools, products, and fail products into a CSV by interpolating raw strings into quoted cells. This is not safe CSV generation: embedded quotes, commas, and line breaks are not escaped according to RFC 4180, and cells beginning with spreadsheet formula metacharacters such as `=`, `+`, `-`, `@`, tab, or carriage return are not neutralized. Craft data is editable by lower-privileged administrators/builders through the craft building commands. A malicious lower-privileged staff member could create a craft value such as a formula payload, wait for a Founder/operator to run the export and open the generated CSV in Excel/LibreOffice/Google Sheets, and trigger spreadsheet formula execution or external data exfiltration depending on the spreadsheet client configuration. The export should use a real CSV writer and additionally prefix or otherwise neutralize formula-leading values before writing cells intended for spreadsheets.

title: Employment task creation misses authority checks
description: The commit introduces action plans whose steps declare required authorities, including financial authorities such as WithdrawBusinessCash. However, CreateScheduledRule only checks CreateScheduledRules, CreateActiveTask only checks AssignTasks, and CreateGoal checks the caller against the caller-supplied ManagerGoalDefinition.RequiredAuthority rather than the actual action plan's RequiredAuthority. EvaluateGoals then creates active tasks with authorisedBy set to null, bypassing even the generic CreateActiveTask check. As a result, any future command or adapter that exposes these APIs to host managers can let a lower-authority manager create scheduled, active, or goal-spawned tasks containing higher-authority actions. Dispatcher/execution still checks the assigned employee's step authority, but the unprivileged creator can queue/coerce actions for any eligible high-authority employee/NPC and persist them for later execution.

title: Unsanitized imported dice faces enable client injection
description: The new tossable-dice importer treats item.DescKeys from the legacy worldfile as dice face names, stores those strings in generated Dice component XML, and the existing roll command displays die.Roll() results to connected clients. The code only splits desc_keys on spaces; it does not reject or strip ANSI escape sequences, OSC sequences, newlines/control characters, or FutureMUD's internal MXP marker bytes. PlayerConnection's MXP sanitizer escapes ordinary '<' and '>' characters but explicitly processes internal MXP marker sequences, so a malicious imported worldfile can persist client-control text in a dice face. After import, any player rolling the dice can cause the payload to be sent to nearby clients, enabling terminal/MXP spoofing or client-side command/interaction injection depending on client support.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMUDItemTransformer.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudItemImporter.cs | MudSharpCore/Commands/Modules/ManipulationModule.cs | FutureMUDLibrary/Framework/MXP.cs

title: NPC importer lets one bad record block all imports
description: FutureMudNpcValidation emits source-keyed errors for individual NPC definitions, such as one unresolved NPC, one missing race/culture, or one missing attribute/AI/name profile. Before this commit, Apply skipped only definitions whose own SourceKey appeared in the error set. The new fatalIssues.Count > 0 check treats the presence of any error anywhere in the batch as fatal for every non-existing, non-deferred definition. As a result, an attacker or untrusted worldfile contributor can add a single invalid NPC record to make apply-npcs import zero otherwise valid NPC templates, and dry-run output will also mislabel valid records as skipped-invalid. This is primarily an offline import availability/integrity issue rather than a live MUD server compromise.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudNpcImporter.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/Program.cs

title: Hard-coded debug database credential added
description: The converter now embeds a default database connection string with uid=futuremud and password=rpiengine2020. When the tool is built in DEBUG mode and no --db-connection/--connection-string is supplied, CreateDatabaseContext silently uses this credential, with SslMode=None and AllowPublicKeyRetrieval=True. Since dotnet run/build commonly defaults to DEBUG, operators or automation can accidentally use a public, hard-coded database credential. If a deployment reuses this credential and the MySQL service is reachable, an attacker who can read the public source can authenticate to the database. Although the repository already has weak database defaults elsewhere, this commit introduces an additional converter-level fallback and broadens accidental use of the known credential.

title: NPC water memory can cause repeated save amplification
description: The commit adds persistent NPC water memories. However, TryDrinkIfThirsty calls RememberCurrentWaterIfPresent when the NPC is not thirsty or is busy, and the forager/predator AIs invoke this logic from frequent tick/event handlers. RememberCurrentWaterIfPresent calls Remember whenever local drinkable liquid exists. Remember always removes/reinserts the cell ID and sets Changed = true, even if the remembered-water list is already identical, such as when the same cell is already first in the list. Since Effect.Changed maps to the owner character's EffectsChanged flag, this queues the character to save and rewrites EffectData. A player who can place open drinkable containers near many affected NPCs, or keep NPCs in water-source rooms, can cause repeated unnecessary database writes after each save cycle. This is primarily an availability/performance issue rather than a privilege, confidentiality, or account-integrity flaw.

title: Form switching can cancel dragging restraints
description: The commit adds the `form` command and body switching flow. The command is only gated on `CharacterState.Conscious`, and `CanSwitchBody` only rejects dead, stasis, same-body, non-owned, non-voluntary, or structurally invalid target bodies. It does not reject characters who are currently being dragged or otherwise subject to general/movement blocking effects. During the switch, `PrepareForBodySwitch` removes `Dragging` and `Dragging.DragTarget` effects. As a result, a player with an allowed alternate form can issue `form <alias>` while being dragged and have the drag state forcibly removed, bypassing the normal movement/control restriction. This is an in-game state/authorization bypass rather than an account/server compromise, and it requires the attacker to have an already-configured voluntarily switchable form.

title: Generated exports disclose local paths and world data
description: This is not a runtime authentication or code-execution vulnerability in the MUD server, but the commit introduces an information disclosure issue by checking in temporary/generated worldfile conversion artifacts. The files include absolute local Windows paths containing the developer username and worktree layout, plus extensive converted world content and audit metadata. If the repository or release archives are shared, this unintentionally discloses internal conversion inputs and potentially unpublished game content/mechanics. These artifacts should be removed from source control and covered by .gitignore or generated into an ignored output directory; any future committed samples should be sanitized.

title: Plaintext MySQL credentials added to launch profile
description: The new `RPI Engine` profile in `MudSharpCore/Properties/launchSettings.json` embeds a full MySQL connection string, including `database=rpi_engine`, `uid=futuremud`, and `password=rpiengine2020`. Because this file is tracked in source control, anyone with repository access can recover these credentials and attempt to use them against matching developer, CI, staging, or accidentally exposed MySQL instances. The connection string points at localhost, which limits direct remote exploitability, and the same password pattern already appears elsewhere in this file, but this commit still introduces an additional hard-coded database target and perpetuates credential disclosure in tracked configuration.

title: NPC importer crashes on XML-invalid mob text
description: RpiNpcWorldfileParser.ReadTildeString returns mob text exactly as read from the ASCII worldfile. FutureMudNpcImporter then embeds ShortDescription, FullDescription/LongDescription, and TechnicalName into XElement/XCData and serializes the template definition with ToString(). XML 1.0 rejects many ASCII control characters, including common terminal escape bytes such as U+001B. If a supplied mobs.* file contains such a byte in a simple NPC description or accepted technical name, BuildNpcTemplate will throw while apply-npcs is processing that record. The importer has no per-record exception handling and saves every 50 inserted templates, so an attacker-controlled corpus used during migration can repeatedly abort NPC import and may leave a partially applied import set.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/RpiNpcWorldfileParser.cs | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/FutureMudNpcImporter.cs

title: Malformed room files can crash room conversion
description: The commit adds room parsing for files matching rooms.*. During directory parsing, every matching filename is sorted by GetZoneNumber before any per-block error handling runs. GetZoneNumber applies an unlimited digit regex to the full path and then calls int.Parse directly. A file named for example rooms.999999999999999999999999 will throw OverflowException during ordering and terminate the converter instead of being reported as a parse failure. The parser also reads each matching room file fully into memory with File.ReadAllLines, so an oversized untrusted room file can cause memory exhaustion. This affects the offline RPI room conversion pipeline rather than the internet-facing MUD server, but it is relevant if operators process room corpora supplied by an untrusted party.

title: Hardcoded legacy MySQL password added
description: The newly added legacy C++ server code initializes default database connection settings with a plaintext MySQL username and password. The accompanying MySQL initialization code then uses these configuration values directly when opening a database connection. Although this code is under the RPI Engine Worldfile Converter's legacy 'Old SOI Code' directory and does not appear to be part of the main FutureMUD production server build, publishing hardcoded credentials can still expose real or reused secrets and can encourage insecure deployments if someone builds or runs the legacy code without overriding the defaults.
relevant_paths: RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/Old SOI Code/src/server.cpp | RPI Engine Worldfile Converter/Old SOI Code/src/mysql.cpp

title: Electronic locks fail open when source binding is invalid
description: ElectronicLockGameItemComponent.ReconnectSource now calls ApplySignalValue(0.0) whenever LocalSignalSinkSubscription cannot resolve the configured source. ApplySignalValue computes shouldLock from that value and calls SetLocked, so with the default 'lock when above threshold 0.5' configuration a missing or non-signal source unlocks the lock. The new prototype source command resolves any item component prototype by id/name and CanSubmit only checks that an id/name exists, not that it is an ISignalSourceComponent that will be present on the same item. This can turn an electronic lock into a fail-open lock after load/reconnect if the configured source is invalid, deleted, replaced, or deliberately set to a non-source prototype.

title: Computer prog filter permits market mutation functions
description: This commit introduces computer FutureProg compilation contexts intended for restricted computer/player-authored programs. However, FunctionCompilerInformation defaults every built-in function to all compilation contexts when no explicit allowedContexts is supplied. ComputerCompilationRestrictions then blocks only a hard-coded set of disallowed categories and otherwise permits functions whose parameter and return types are among the computer-safe scalar/container types. The existing market influence functions are categorized as "Markets", which is not in the disallowed category list, and their signatures use only Number, Text, MudDateTime, TimeSpan, and Boolean. As a result, a computer-context program can compile calls such as BeginInfluence(marketId, templateId) or EndInfluence(influenceId). Those functions have persistent game-world side effects: BeginInfluence constructs a MarketInfluence and adds it to the gameworld, while EndInfluence cancels an existing influence. If/when the computer program authoring/execution path is exposed to players as designed, this lets a low-privileged computer-program author mutate the economic subsystem by numeric IDs, bypassing the intended computer sandbox.

title: MSBuild binlog exposes developer environment metadata
description: The new mudcoretests.binlog file is a compressed MSBuild binary log. Inspecting it shows diagnostic build metadata such as C:\Users\luker\OneDrive\source\repos\FutureMUD paths, C:\Users\luker AppData locations, installed SDK/workload paths, command-line arguments, and DOTNET_CLI_TELEMETRY_SESSIONID. I did not find database, SMTP, Discord, OAuth, or other production secrets in this specific binlog, so the impact appears limited to low-sensitivity developer/environment disclosure. However, committing binlogs is risky because MSBuild binary logs commonly capture environment variables, properties, command lines, and imported project data that may include secrets in other environments.

title: Committed MSBuild diagnostic log leaks developer metadata
description: The new root-level dbseeder-build-diag.log file is a UTF-16 MSBuild diagnostic log. It includes environment and build metadata including the developer Windows username, AppData paths, current worktree path, .NET SDK/MSBuild versions, and DOTNET_CLI_TELEMETRY_SESSIONID. I did not find database passwords, API keys, or connection strings in the log, so the practical impact is limited; however, committing diagnostic logs is an information disclosure footgun and can aid targeted attacks or leak more sensitive values in future logs.

title: Seeder can create fireplaces that burn any item
description: SeedModernItems looks up a Fuel tag but unconditionally creates SolidFuelHeaterCooler components. CreateSolidFuelHeaterCooler writes fuelTag?.Id ?? 0 into the component XML, so worlds that choose the modern item package without installing the tags package receive solid-fuel thermal components with FuelTag=0. SolidFuelHeaterCoolerGameItemComponentProto loads that as a null FuelTag. At runtime, SolidFuelHeaterCoolerGameItemComponent.CanPut calls item.IsA(_prototype.FuelTag), and GameItem.IsA(null) returns true. Once switched on, BurnFuel deletes the selected contained item. This makes these seeded components broader and more destructive than their descriptions imply.

title: Committed diagnostic build log leaks developer environment
description: The new build-thermal.log file is a generated build artifact and should not be committed. Its early lines disclose the developer Windows username, local worktree path, NuGet/MSBuild directories, .NET SDK/MSBuild versions, and a DOTNET_CLI_TELEMETRY_SESSIONID. I did not find database passwords, API tokens, or application secrets in the log, so the direct impact is limited. However, publishing local environment details can still aid fingerprinting, targeted phishing/social engineering, or tailoring attacks against the developer/build environment.

title: Committed build log leaks local environment metadata
description: The new dbseed-test.log file is a generated diagnostic build artifact. It records the build environment and MSBuild properties, including C:\Users\luker paths, DOTNET_CLI_HOME, DOTNET_CLI_TELEMETRY_SESSIONID, SDK locations, and project working directories. I did not find database passwords, API keys, SMTP credentials, or Discord tokens in the file, so the impact is limited to environment and workstation metadata exposure. However, committing diagnostic logs is unsafe because these files commonly capture secrets in real developer or CI environments and can disclose internal usernames, filesystem layout, tooling versions, and repository paths to anyone with access to the source tree.

title: Alias lookup can shadow canonical clan names
description: The new All<T>.GetByName/GetByIdOrName logic now treats every IHaveMultipleNames.Names entry as an equivalent exact name and returns the first object in list order. This was added for material aliases, but it also affects existing multi-name types such as clans. Clan.Names includes Name, FullName, and Alias, while the clan alias edit path only checks that the new alias is unique among aliases; it does not prevent setting an alias equal to another clan's FullName. As a result, if an attacker with clan-detail editing privileges sets their clan alias to a victim clan's full name, and the attacker's clan appears earlier in the All<IClan> list, actor.Gameworld.Clans.GetByName("Victim Clan") can return the attacker's clan instead of the exact canonical victim clan. Economy flows such as estate heir/bequest or ownership resolution use this helper, so a user or administrator entering an exact clan name could be misdirected to the attacker's clan. This is a low-severity integrity/spoofing issue because it requires in-game clan edit privileges and user interaction, and impact is limited to commands that rely on this generic clan lookup.

title: Diagnostic build log leaks developer environment details
description: The new tracked file mudsharpcore-diag.log is a verbose MSBuild diagnostic log. Such logs commonly capture environment variables and local build metadata, and this instance includes the developer's Windows username, repository path, DOTNET_CLI_HOME, USERPROFILE-derived paths, SDK versions, command-line arguments, and DOTNET_CLI_TELEMETRY_SESSIONID. Even though I did not find DB/SMTP/Discord credentials in the sampled sensitive-string searches, committing diagnostic logs is security-relevant because future or full logs can expose secrets and this one already discloses operational/developer environment details useful for targeted attacks or social engineering. The combat-setting code changes themselves did not reveal a direct remote authentication, authorization, or code-execution flaw during review.

title: Committed build logs leak developer machine identifiers
description: Alongside the command parsing changes, this commit adds generated local build artifacts such as build-diag.log and .dotnet-cli/.dotnet cache files. These files are not needed by the application and disclose developer/environment metadata, including C:\Users\Luke paths, the local source checkout path, USERPROFILE, and persistent .NET machine identifiers. This does not appear to expose application secrets or provide a direct FutureMUD runtime attack path, but if the repository is public or redistributed it creates an avoidable information leak useful for fingerprinting or social-engineering the developer/build host.

title: AI classifier errors can inject Discord admin alerts
description: Room echo text is untrusted player-controlled content and is sent as the user message to the OpenAI attention classifier. If a player prompt-injects the classifier into returning valid JSON with an invalid Decision value containing Discord markdown such as triple backticks and mentions, the parser includes that decision value verbatim in an error string. The new TryInterpretAttentionClassifierOutput path then sends the error through LogStorytellerError, which wraps it in a Discord code block without escaping backticks or suppressing mentions. This lets a player in a surveilled room, when the storyteller is unpaused and GPT is configured, spoof or spam Discord admin notifications. The attack is not deterministic because it depends on LLM compliance, but the commit introduced a new attacker-influenced error-to-Discord path.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Seeded surgery specials silently disable safety checks
description: The new HealthSeeder data seeds phases such as "checkorgan exposed", "exposed checkbone", and "exposed checkspace". At runtime, SurgicalProcedure.LoadFromDB calls GetSpecialPhaseAction once with the entire PhaseSpecialEffects string. The relevant handlers compare the full string to exact values such as "exposed" or "checkspace"; if no handler matches, the base implementation returns a predicate that always succeeds. As a result, these seeded procedures do not enforce their intended phase restrictions. The clearest integrity impact is Install Implant: its "exposed checkspace" phase does not invoke CheckSpacePhaseSpecialAction, so implant capacity can be bypassed if this optional seeder is enabled and run. The HealthSeeder is disabled by default, so this is not active in default runtime deployments, but it is a shipped seeder data bug that can affect operators who enable/use it.

title: Lawful drop check runs after item split side effects
description: In the weighted drop path, the commit calls targetItem.DropByWeight(actor.Location, amount) before calling HandleCrimesAndLawfulActing for littering. DropByWeight is not a pure preview operation: for commodities it creates a new game item, reduces the original commodity weight, assigns the new item a location, logs it in, and fires load events; for stackables it also splits the stack. If littering would be a crime and the player's ActLawfully flag is enabled, HandleCrimesAndLawfulActing returns true and the command returns immediately, so actor.Body.Drop is never called. This leaves side effects from a command that was supposedly blocked. Because the drop-weight regex accepts zero-valued weights, a player in a jurisdiction where littering is automatically a crime may be able to repeatedly attempt zero-weight commodity drops and create initialized but uninserted/hidden item records without consuming the source commodity, causing database or save-queue bloat.

title: Invalid invisibility prog falls back to always-true
description: AddInvisEffectFunction previously returned failure when the supplied prog ID/name could not be resolved. In this commit, the lookup result is coalesced to _gameworld.AlwaysTrueProg. Because the seeded AlwaysTrue prog accepts any parameters and always returns true, the following signature check passes, an Invis effect is added, and that effect obscures all perception types. Any FutureProg path that accepts an untrusted or user-controlled prog reference, or relies on an invalid reference meaning no invisibility, can now be coerced into granting persistent unconditional invisibility to a perceivable.

title: Plaintext database credentials added to launch settings
description: MudSharpCore/Properties/launchSettings.json is committed to the repository and this commit adds a new "Gestalt" profile containing a plaintext MySQL connection string with database name, username, and password. Anyone with access to the repository can recover these credentials. If operators reuse this credential or if the referenced database is reachable from an attacker-controlled environment, the attacker may be able to access or modify game database contents. The connection string points to localhost, so this is not directly exploitable against a properly isolated production host, but it is still a source-controlled secret and unsafe deployment footgun.

title: Covered implants leak to ordinary viewers
description: In BodyPerception.LookText, the patch separates all external implants from the subset on exposed bodyparts. However, once the output condition is satisfied, the displayed list is built from externalImplants rather than from a viewer-authorized list. For ordinary non-admin viewers, the condition is true when at least one external implant is visible, and then the code displays every external implant, marking covered ones as "[Covered]". This leaks the existence, description, and bodypart location of covered implants to players who should only see implants on exposed bodyparts. The intended behavior appears to be: admins and the target see all external implants with covered markers, while other viewers see only visibleExternalImplants.

title: Combo merit children can be granted multiple times
description: In the Body constructor, combo merit children are filtered to MeritScope.Character and then added to Body._merits. The Character constructor independently performs the same MeritScope.Character child expansion into Character._merits. Because Character.Merits concatenates Character._merits with Body.Merits, the same child merit can appear twice for a newly approved character. For trait-bonus merits this is worse because Body.TraitValue separately sums Body.Merits and Actor.Merits, so a character-scoped child stored on the body can be counted more than once. A player who can select an affected role or combo merit in character generation could receive stronger-than-intended skills/traits after approval. The nearby TraitDefinition change appears to fix the approval-time crash by avoiding an early Id-triggered save for unregistered late-initialising owners; the security-relevant concern found in this patch is the introduced duplicate merit grant.

title: Antiquity food vessel seeding can fail validation
description: SeedAntiquityFoodCrafts now calls SeedAntiquityFoodVesselCrafts. Both new vessel crafts are added through AddAntiquityCraft with SimpleFoodPhases(...), which returns exactly two craft phases. AddAntiquityCraft hard-codes failPhase to 3 when forwarding to AddCraft. Craft validation rejects any fail phase greater than the number of phases, so a fresh antiquity seeding run that reaches these crafts should raise an ApplicationException instead of completing. This is an operator-facing DatabaseSeeder availability/functional bug, not a remote security vulnerability.

title: Duplicate leather quiver craft name skips one seed
description: The new container craft list contains both `antiquity_accessory_common_tall_arrow_quiver` and `antiquity_accessory_kushite_tall_arrow_quiver` with the identical name `assemble a tall leather arrow quiver`. All of these crafts are added in the `Leatherworking` category. The seeder's `AddCraft` path checks for an existing craft by name and category and returns it without creating a new craft. As a result, when the loop reaches the Kushite tall quiver entry, it will find the earlier common tall quiver craft and skip creating the separate Kushite product/material recipe. This is a content/data seeding bug, not a security vulnerability.

title: Leather assembly crafts are seeded invalid
description: The new `AddAntiquityLeatherCraft` helper ultimately passes an empty fail-product list when callers omit the optional `failProducts` argument. The finished leather assembly phase templates for clothing, armour, containers, liquid vessels, and furnishings all include fail echoes such as `salvages only $f1`, but their calls only provide normal products and do not pass a fail-product list. In `Craft.SetupPhaseInformation`, any `$fN` reference in a fail echo is validated against `_orderedFailProducts`; when no matching fail product exists, `_craftIsValid` is set to false. `AppearInCraftsList` then requires `_craftIsValid`, making these stock crafts unavailable to players. This is a functional data/availability bug for the new craft suite, not a security vulnerability.

title: Unlimited clan appointments can no longer be filled
description: Appointment maximum holder count 0 is explicitly documented and handled by the builder command as meaning unlimited. Before this commit, ClanCommandUtilities.HasFreePosition returned true whenever MaximumSimultaneousHolders was less than 1. The refactor now routes HasFreePosition through GetVacantAppointmentSlots, but that helper returns 0 for MaximumSimultaneousHolders < 1. As a result, Clan.Appoint sees FreePosition as false and rejects appointing any additional member to an unlimited appointment. This is a functional authorization/governance regression rather than a direct security vulnerability, because it blocks legitimate clan appointment assignment but does not by itself let an attacker gain privileges or access data.

title: Predator AI paths to food it cannot consume
description: In the refactored PredatorFeedingStrategy.GetPath, a hungry predator now first searches for scavenger food and then for forager food before looking for attackable prey. However, PredatorFeedingStrategy.TrySatisfyImmediateNeed still only calls PredatorAIHelpers.EatLocalCorpseIfHungry, and HasLocalFoodOpportunity only considers edible corpses or attackable characters. As a result, a pure predator can path toward foraging opportunities or non-corpse edible items, arrive there, fail to eat them, and continue repeating pathing work instead of hunting. This appears to be a logic regression from the refactor rather than a security vulnerability.

title: Obsolete NPC AI rows can break startup after upgrade
description: FutureMUD loads every ArtificialIntelligence row from the database at startup and dispatches by exact Type using _AITypeLoaders[ai.Type]. This commit deletes the HungryAggressor, TerritorialPredator, DenningPredator, TerritorialForager, and DenningForager classes that previously registered those Type strings, while the new AnimalAI only registers the new Animal type. An existing world database containing any of the removed AI Type values will now throw a KeyNotFoundException during startup and fail to load unless the operator manually migrates or deletes those rows first. This is primarily an upgrade compatibility/availability regression rather than a direct new remote security vulnerability.

title: Blocked warded spells still apply caster effects
description: The commit adds magic interdiction handling in MagicSpell.CastSpell. When a ward blocks a spell, ProcessSpellTarget echoes the block and returns false. That false value is also used for the normal non-aborting path, so the caller continues and later applies _casterSpellEffects to the magician. As a result, a player can cast a spell at a warded target or into a warded room, have the target portion blocked, and still receive any caster-side effects. This undermines the intended semantics of wards as blocking the spell invocation.

title: Spell poison origin is lost across saves
description: SpellPoisonEffect applies a body drug dosage with the effect instance itself as the originator and later removes only dosages whose Originator is the same object reference. The commit also changed Body.Dose and drug ticking to separate otherwise-identical drug dosages by Originator. However, existing body drug save/load logic still persists only drug id, active flag, vector, and grams, and reloads DrugDosage objects without any Originator. As a result, a persisted spell poison can lose ownership after a save/load cycle. When the MagicSpellParent reloads, the child SpellPoisonEffect is re-added and InitialEffect doses the character again with the new effect object as originator, while the old DB-loaded dosage remains originator-less. Later removal of the spell only removes the new object-owned dosage, leaving the old poison behind. This can make spell poison persist beyond its configured spell lifetime and can duplicate dosage across restarts.

title: Generic builder commands broken for some item components
description: Several changed components inherit through abstract helper bases that do not override BuildingCommand, so their fallback call reaches GameItemComponentProto.BuildingCommand directly. GameItemComponentProto does not pop the command token; it switches on command.Last, which is populated by the caller's earlier PopSpeech(). Replacing the original stack with command.GetUndo() creates a fresh StringStack whose Memory is empty and whose Last is null. As a result, commands such as name, desc/description, and keywords no longer work on affected direct-base components, including the changed thermal, door, and locking-door components. This appears to be a builder/OLC regression rather than a security vulnerability.

title: Upgrade crash loading legacy automation sensors
description: This commit converts MotionSensorGameItemComponent and TimerSensorGameItemComponent to derive from PoweredMachineBaseGameItemComponent. Their database-loading constructors now invoke the powered-machine loader, whose LoadFromXml unconditionally dereferences root.Element("SwitchedOn").Value. Existing persisted MotionSensor and TimerSensor component definitions created before this commit only saved ActiveUntilTicks or CycleAnchorTicks/StartsActive, so they lack SwitchedOn. Loading such a world after upgrade will throw a NullReferenceException before the item finishes loading. This is primarily a robustness/availability and migration bug rather than an externally exploitable security issue under the stated threat model, but it can prevent a production game from starting when it has legitimate pre-existing automation sensor items.

title: Duplicate tattoo names overwrite stock templates
description: The commit adds a large list of stock tattoo definitions, but at least two names are duplicated: "Butterfly" and "Paired Swallows". The seeder resolves templates by case-insensitive type/name and then assigns the current definition fields onto that single database row. As a result, the later "Butterfly" definition overwrites the earlier chargen-selectable "Butterfly" definition, removing its CanSelectInChargen setting and changing its bodypart aliases. This is a content/data integrity bug rather than a security vulnerability.

title: OldSun rows now abort startup without migration
description: FuturemudLoaders now throws an ApplicationException whenever a persisted celestial has CelestialType == "OldSun". However, the new migration only alters the default value for future Celestials.CelestialType inserts from OldSun to Sun. It does not update existing rows or transform legacy OldSun definitions into the NewSun format. Because older migrations explicitly created the column with defaultValue "OldSun", existing deployments can realistically contain OldSun records. After applying this commit, those worlds will abort while loading celestials, preventing the server from starting until the operator manually repairs the database.

title: Lensmaking tags are orphaned by seeder order
description: The seeder resolves a tag's parent immediately when AddTag is called. In the newly added tag expansion, "Lensmaking Tools" is added with parent "Scientific Tools" at line 948, but "Scientific Tools" is not added until line 1020. On a fresh database where "Scientific Tools" is not already present, the dictionary lookup returns the default value, so "Lensmaking Tools" is stored with a null parent. Adding "Scientific Tools" later does not repair that relationship. This can break tag hierarchy semantics for lensmaking-related items or recipes, but it is an operator-run seed-data integrity issue rather than a security vulnerability.

title: No security vulnerability; minor telephone connector mismatch
description: The changed telephone prototype constructor now creates a male connector of type "TelephoneLine". However, the telecommunications outlet prototype's default constructor and fallback XML loading path still create female connectors of type "telephone". Connector compatibility requires the connection type strings to match case-insensitively, so "TelephoneLine" and "telephone" are not compatible. This affects builder-created default components, not the newly seeded modern item package, because the seeder explicitly creates both the stock telephone and stock telecommunications outlet with "TelephoneLine". I did not find any authentication, authorization, information disclosure, code execution, or remotely triggerable denial-of-service issue introduced by this commit.

title: Seeder status check can crash on nullable market data
description: The changed EconomySeeder.ShouldSeedData logic now enumerates MarketInfluenceTemplates and MarketCategories in process before calling StartsWith. The relevant database columns are nullable longtext columns, so rows with NULL Name or Description values can cause NullReferenceException during the seeder assessment menu. Previously, the MarketCategories Description predicate was evaluated by the database, where NULL values would not match rather than crashing. This is a robustness regression in an operator-run seeding tool, not an internet-exposed security vulnerability.

title: Codex sandbox widened to host package and .NET directories
description: The new `.codex/config.toml` entries expand `workspace-write` permissions to absolute host paths including NuGet package/config locations and .NET/AppData directories. If a developer runs Codex using this repository configuration, a malicious prompt, prompt-injected instructions, or compromised agent action could write outside the project tree and poison package caches/configuration or SDK-related files. This is not a FutureMUD runtime vulnerability exploitable by normal MUD clients, but it is a supply-chain/developer-environment integrity issue introduced by this commit.

title: Downed cleave message uses blank bodypart placeholder
description: The new `Zweihander 2-Handed Downed Cleave` seed data uses `$1's {0}` in a `BuiltInCombatMoveType.DownedAttack` weapon message. Runtime formatting for melee weapon attacks calls `string.Format(..., "", TargetBodypart.FullDescription())`, so `{0}` expands to an empty string and `{1}` is the target bodypart. As a result, this seeded combat message will render malformed text such as an attack at the defender's blank bodypart. This does not appear to create a crash or security boundary issue because the placeholder index is in range, but it is an introduced stock-content formatting bug. The message should use `{1}` instead of `{0}`.

title: No security impact in job economic-zone changes
description: The changed code adds optional economic-zone parsing inside job creation/show paths, and updates help text to advertise an optional <ez> argument. However, the public Job command still performs a current-location economic-zone check before dispatching to JobNew, JobNewClan, JobShow, or JobEdit. If the actor is not already at a job-finding cell, the command returns early, so the new fallback branches that parse an economic-zone name when ez is null cannot be reached through normal command dispatch. Additionally, JobEdit help advertises an optional economic-zone argument, but JobEdit does not parse one and still rejects actors outside a job-posting location. This is a command behavior/documentation bug, not a security boundary bypass.

title: No security issue; seeder can duplicate sample items
description: The changed filter for the UsefulSeeder item package now unconditionally returns true. The seeder UI uses this filter to decide whether to ask the item package question, and a yes answer runs all four item seeding stages. Component prototypes are mostly protected by AddGameItemComponent, which reuses existing component definitions by name. However, SeedItemsPart1 also unconditionally creates and saves three GameItemProto rows for A4, A3, and A5 paper without checking whether equivalent prototypes already exist. On an already-seeded database, answering yes to the now-always-visible item question can therefore add duplicate paper item prototypes. This is an operator-triggered seeder/data-quality issue, not a remotely exploitable security flaw.

title: Migration ID replacement can crash upgraded servers
description: The new `20260309072751_WeatherModelSimplification` migration adds the same weather/climate columns that were previously added by the now-deleted `20260309093000_WeatherClimateRuntimeSimplification` migration. EF Core tracks applied migrations by migration ID in `__EFMigrationsHistory`, not by schema contents. Therefore, an operator who ran the previous commit and already has `20260309093000_WeatherClimateRuntimeSimplification` recorded will upgrade to this commit with the columns already present, but EF will consider `20260309072751_WeatherModelSimplification` pending. Because the server and seeder automatically apply all pending migrations, they will attempt to add already-existing columns and throw an exception, preventing startup or seeding. This is an operational availability bug, not an attacker-controlled security vulnerability.

title: Arena cleanup skips cached participants' no-quit effects
description: Before this commit, ClearParticipation(IArenaEvent) walked the event's participant list and resolved each participant character, including cached/offline characters. The new implementation instead scans only _gameworld.Actors. In this codebase, Actors is explicitly only the collection of actors loaded into the game, while cached offline characters live separately in CachedActors and can still be returned by TryGetCharacter(..., true). Arena phase code can operate on participant.Character and add participation effects to such cached participants. If an event later completes or aborts while that character is cached rather than in Actors, the new cleanup path will not remove the ArenaParticipationEffect. The added Login() self-prune does not reliably compensate because arena participation effects are added unscheduled, and effect Login() is only invoked for scheduled cached effects during character login. The result is a stale INoQuitEffect/INoTimeOutEffect that can continue blocking quit/timeout for the affected character until reboot/reload or staff cleanup.

title: Prompt-injected room emotes can drive AI tools
description: When AI Storyteller room subscriptions are enabled, every emote output in watched cells is passed to Cell_OnRoomEcho. That method sends the parsed room echo to the attention model, and if the model returns "interested", PassSituationToAIStoryteller sends the same attacker-influenced echo to a tool-enabled AI conversation. The newly added tools include OnlinePlayers, PlayerInformation, CreateMemory, UpdateMemory, and situation-management functions. The server then executes any function calls returned by the model directly, including writing persistent AI memories/situations and returning detailed player information, without checking whether the originating player is authorized, whether the target character is the originator, or whether the tool request came from trusted instructions rather than prompt-injected room text. A normal player in a monitored room can craft an emote/speech prompt such as instructions to call CreateMemory or PlayerInformation for another character, turning the LLM into a confused deputy. This can corrupt persistent storyteller state and, if the tool loop is made to work as intended, expose true descriptions/locations/custom player information to the external AI context.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: Cylinder autobuilder toggles are not persisted
description: The commit adds two editable settings to AutobuilderAreaCylinder: ConnectCellsWithDiagonalExits and ConnectRingsAlongLengthOfCylinder. Their BuildingCommand handlers toggle the backing properties and return success, but unlike other saveable autobuilder settings they do not set Changed = true. SaveableItem only queues an object for persistence when Changed is set, and GenericSet does not perform any unconditional save. As a result, an admin can believe they enabled cylinder linking/diagonals, but those changes are lost after restart or save cycle unless another edit happens to mark the object changed.

title: TimeSpan compound assignments compile but fail at runtime
description: The commit adds compound assignment operators for FutureProg. In PlusEqualsVariable and MinusEqualsVariable, the compiler explicitly permits TimeSpan variables and accepts a compatible TimeSpan RHS. However, the Execute methods only handle collections, DateTime, MudDateTime, Number, and Text. A valid prog such as `duration += other_duration` or `duration -= other_duration` will compile successfully, then fall through to `throw new ApplicationException("Unknown += type in Prog")` or `throw new ApplicationException("Unknown -= type in Prog")` at runtime. In production this is likely caught by FutureProg's top-level exception handling and reported as a prog failure, but it is still an introduced stability/correctness bug and may break game logic that relies on compiled progs executing successfully.

title: GetPath returns raw strings in text collection
description: FutureProg collections are later treated as collections of IProgVariable instances. The new GetPath implementation builds a List<string> from path directions and passes it to CollectionVariable as a TEXT collection. Operations such as foreach, reverse, select, where, or accessing collection members can cast those string entries to IProgVariable, causing invalid casts or null values at runtime. The null/invalid-parameter branches also return BooleanVariable(false) despite the function advertising a text collection return type. This appears to be an introduced correctness/stability bug rather than a direct security vulnerability, because FutureProgs are admin-authored and production execution catches unhandled prog exceptions, but an admin-authored/player-triggered prog using getpath could reliably fail and generate error notifications.

title: No security vulnerability found in this commit
description: The project-level target framework changes do not themselves introduce a directly exploitable security issue. The meaningful code change renames the custom RandomUtilities.Shuffle methods to OldShuffle so most call sites now bind to .NET 10's System.Linq.Enumerable.Shuffle. That is not security-sensitive in the inspected paths. However, RandomTruthMask still calls bools.Shuffle() and ignores the returned enumerable. Under .NET 10 this no longer mutates the array in place, so the truth mask is not shuffled. This affects language scrambling by making masked words deterministic rather than randomly distributed, but it does not appear to compromise accounts, authorization, secrets, persistence integrity, or remote code execution boundaries.

title: Item FutureProg quality fields are miswired
description: The added FutureProg properties document separate effective quality and raw/base quality values. However, the GetProperty implementation wires two of the new properties to the wrong backing values. The numeric quality property correctly returns Quality, but qualityname returns RawQuality.DescribeEnum(), so quality and qualityname can disagree. Conversely, rawquality returns (int)Quality, while rawqualityname returns RawQuality.DescribeEnum(), so rawquality and rawqualityname can also disagree. This matters when an item's effective Quality differs from RawQuality due to skins or IAffectQuality components.

title: No security vulnerability found in firearm refactor
description: The refactor moves shared firearm behavior into FirearmBaseGameItemComponent, which stores its own private prototype reference. The concrete firearm components still override UpdateComponentNewPrototype, but they only update their concrete _prototype fields and do not call the base implementation. As a result, shared base behavior such as CanWield and WhyCannotWield can keep using stale prototype data after an in-place prototype revision update. This can cause existing firearm instances to ignore live changes to shared firearm settings until reload/recreation. This appears to be a gameplay/state-consistency bug rather than a security boundary violation in the reviewed threat model.

title: Arena seeder creates invalid FutureProgs
description: The commit adds ArenaSeeder, which creates three baseline FutureProgs using FunctionText values of "return true;" and "return;". Existing FutureProg syntax does not use semicolon terminators: the return statement parser accepts a bare "return" or "return" followed by whitespace and an expression. As a result, "return;" does not match the return statement at all, and "return true;" passes "true;" to the expression parser, where bool.TryParse rejects it. These seeded progs will therefore fail compilation when the server loads FutureProgs, leaving the example arena with invalid eligibility/outfit/intro programs. This is a functional seeder/runtime configuration bug rather than an exploitable security issue because the input is local operator-seeded content and no attacker-controlled privilege boundary is crossed.

title: Default Elo grants wins to both sides when winner is missing
description: ApplyDefaultElo allows Outcome=Win to proceed even when WinningSides is null or empty. ComputeActualScore then special-cases two-sided events with an empty winning set and returns 1.0 for every side. For an even two-sided match this makes both sides receive a positive Elo delta, corrupting rating integrity. IArenaEvent does not require a WinningSides property, and the service treats that metadata as optional, so events that expose an outcome but omit winners will be rated incorrectly.

title: Pari-mutuel payout can overpay multi-winner events
description: In Settle, every active bet whose SideIndex appears in winningSides is paid independently. After this commit, CalculatePayout computes each winning bet's share using totalPool, which is the sum of all side pools, but still divides only by that bet's own side pool. This is correct for a single winning side, but if winningSides contains multiple side indexes, the code distributes the full net total pool once per winning side. For example, with two winning sides and equal take rates, side A bettors receive approximately 90% of the total pool and side B bettors also receive approximately 90% of the total pool, producing approximately 180% total payout. That can drain arena reserves or force blocked payouts, and colluding players could exploit it in pari-mutuel multi-winner events.

title: Combat movement check bonuses invert speed outcomes
description: The new CombatMovementSpeedExpression default is `baseSpeed * (1 + (0.1 * degree))`. `degree` is positive for successful checks and negative for failed checks. However, the existing movement code treats `MoveSpeed` as a delay/time value: lower values are faster and win pursuit comparisons. As a result, a MajorPass applies a 1.3x delay, slowing the character, while a MajorFail applies a 0.7x delay, making the character faster. This affects the new flee and pursuit speed checks in combat movement and reverses the intended reward/penalty semantics.

title: Mounted riders can bypass stamina when mount cannot move
description: The changed code skips both the pre-move stamina gate and the stamina deduction whenever `RidingMount` is non-null. That is safe only if the mount is always included in the movement and always validated as the stamina-paying mover. However, `EvaluateCharacterForAdditionToMovement` recursively evaluates a rider's mount but ignores the recursive return value. If the mount fails `CanMove` because it is exhausted or otherwise unable to move, the rider evaluation can still return success and add the rider as a non-dragger. The later intermediate validation also skips non-draggers whose `RidingMount` is non-null, so the rider can execute movement without the mount and without paying stamina. This appears to be a gameplay/integrity bug rather than a security vulnerability under the provided threat model.

title: Seeded lockable doors use mismatched key type
description: The new CreateLockableDoor helper writes the supplied lockType directly into the LockType XML element, and every new lockable door template passes "Warded_Lock". Existing seeded warded locks and the Warded_Key use "Warded Lock" with a space. SimpleKeyGameItemComponent.Unlocks only returns true when the key and lock LockType strings are equal ignoring case; it does not normalize underscores/spaces. As a result, the newly seeded lockable doors are not compatible with the default warded key templates. This appears to be a functional/content seeding bug rather than a security vulnerability: it does not expose authentication, permissions, secrets, or remote code execution, and it is only triggered if an operator/builder uses these seeded templates.

title: Mounted-combat checks are inverted
description: Two new mount-related checks use the opposite condition from the surrounding logic. In the magic attack defense path, canDodge is true only when defender.RidingMount is not null, while the analogous melee and ranged paths disable dodging when mounted. This means unmounted defenders may be unable to dodge dodgeable magic attacks, while mounted defenders may incorrectly dodge them. In the release-kneel path, the command rejects targets whose RidingMount is null while the message says mounted targets cannot be forced to kneel; this blocks normal unmounted targets and allows mounted targets to be set kneeling while still mounted. These are exploitable gameplay bugs but do not cross an authentication, authorization, confidentiality, or server availability boundary.

title: New AI handlers compare character IDs to AI IDs
description: NPC.HandleEvent dispatches events to the AI instances attached to that NPC. The new handlers then independently test whether the event character or witness has an Id equal to `Id` inherited from ArtificialIntelligenceBase. That `Id` is the database Id of the AI definition, not the NPC character Id. As a result, IdleEmoterAI and most ReactAI reactions only run when an NPC character happens to have the same numeric Id as the AI record. This is a functional availability/correctness bug in the newly introduced AI behavior, not an exploitable security issue in the server's authentication, authorization, or data handling.

title: Merit prog functions ignore failed merit mutations
description: The commit adds FutureProg functions for granting and removing merits. Character.Merits includes both character merits and body merits, but Character.AddMerit only accepts MeritScope.Character and Character.RemoveMerit only removes from the character-level _merits list. GiveMeritFunction ignores the boolean return from target.AddMerit and always returns true after calling it. RemoveMeritFunction checks target.Merits.Contains, which can be true for body merits, then ignores the false return from target.RemoveMerit and reports true. This can make progs believe a merit was granted or removed when no state change occurred. The impact appears to be game-logic/integrity correctness rather than a security vulnerability because FutureProg editing is Admin-gated in this tree.

title: Mind broadcast distance is not persisted
description: MindBroadcastPower.SaveDefinition serializes the new PowerDistance value under the XML element name "Distance". The load constructor, however, looks for "PowerDistance" and falls back to enum value 2 when it is missing. As a result, any distance configured with the new builder command appears to work only until the object is reloaded from the database; after a server restart or reload it silently becomes SameLocationOnly. This is a functional persistence bug in the newly introduced range feature, not a clear security vulnerability.

title: SHOW CHARACTER reads description patterns from wrong object
description: The new SHOW CHARACTER output attempts to report description pattern IDs using Character._shortDescriptionPattern and Character._fullDescriptionPattern. Character.HowSeen delegates description rendering to Body.HowSeen, and Body loads and exposes the actual description patterns. Because Character's inherited pattern fields are not populated in the normal load/creation path, SHOW CHARACTER will generally print "None" even when the character's Body has active short/full description patterns. This is not security-impacting, but it defeats the stated purpose of the commit.

title: Overbroad inventory-position proximity check
description: The new GetProximity branch returns Proximity.Intimate for any non-null queried perceivable whenever the character's PositionTarget is an IGameItem whose LocationLevelPerceivable is the character. It does not verify that the queried `thing` is that inventory item or is otherwise related to it. The new InternalInVicinity override has the same shape: if recursion reaches any inventory item whose top-level perceivable is this character, it returns true regardless of the requested target. If a character can enter or retain a state where PositionTarget points at one of their inventory items, all other room items/characters may be treated as intimate/immediate or in-vicinity for game logic. I did not find a direct normal player command path that sets a character's PositionTarget to a held item, so this appears to be a correctness bug with limited security impact rather than a practical authentication, privilege, or secret-exposure vulnerability.

title: whycantwield none clears CanWield instead
description: The new `BuildingCommandWhyCannotWieldProg` methods are intended to remove the custom error-message FutureProg when a builder runs `whycantwield none`. Instead, they assign `CanWieldProg = null`. This leaves the message prog in place and unintentionally removes the actual wield-eligibility prog. In practice this can accidentally make an item wieldable by actors who should have been denied by the CanWield prog. I do not consider this security-impacting under the stated threat model because these are builder/staff-only OLC commands, and anyone with permission to run this subcommand can also intentionally run `canwield none` to remove the same restriction. It is nevertheless an introduced functional/authorization-logic bug in game content editing.

title: Broken XML loader for DoorguardOpeningDoor effect
description: DoorguardOpeningDoor now saves its exit as <Definition><Exit>...</Exit></Definition>, and Effect.SaveToXml wraps that under the outer <Effect> element. However, the new XML constructor receives the outer <Effect> element and tries to read element.Element("Exit").Value directly. Its own saved format would therefore produce a null dereference during effect loading. In the current normal runtime this effect does not override SavingEffect, so it is not normally persisted through SaveEffects, which keeps this as a robustness/crash bug rather than an identified exploitable security issue.

title: No security vulnerability found in commit
description: The changed bone internal-location command now stores the parsed percentage differently in memory and in the database. TryParsePercentage returns fractional values for inputs such as "50%" or "0.5". The in-memory BodypartInternalInfo is assigned that raw fractional value, while the database value is assigned hitchance * 100.0. Runtime combat logic treats BodypartInternalInfo.HitChance as a percent-scale value and divides by 100.0, so a newly configured 50% bone hit chance becomes 0.5% until the game reloads from the database. This is a gameplay/data-consistency bug in a privileged world-building command, not an authentication, authorization, information leak, or remote availability vulnerability.

title: No security issue; torank rank-number overload typo
description: The new ToClanRankFunction execution path clearly expects a non-text second argument so it can cast the value to a decimal and compare it against IRank.RankNumber. However, the newly added compiler registration for the documented rank-number overload duplicates the existing Clan/Text signature instead of registering Clan/Number. As a result, calls intended to use a numeric rank number will not select this new code path; they may fail to compile or resolve to a different pre-existing torank compiler with different semantics. This is a functional regression/documentation mismatch, not an exploitable security issue based on the changed code.

title: Shard sniff lists zone variables instead of shard variables
description: The new `SniffShard` helper is intended to show debug data for the actor's current shard, including registered FutureProg variables. However, it calls `AllVariables(ProgVariableTypes.Zone)` and then reads those variable names from the shard object. This means `sniff shard` will list zone variable definitions rather than shard definitions, causing shard-only variables not to be shown and same-named variables to be confusingly labeled with the zone variable type. This appears to be a functional bug, not a security issue: the command remains gated by `PermissionLevel.Admin`, and the defect does not create an authentication bypass, privilege escalation, or attacker-controlled crash based on the inspected code.
triage_group: AI Storyteller, Discord, Email & External Integrations

title: GiveAccent can falsely skip granting accents
description: GiveAccentFunction is intended to add the specified accent and return false only if the character already had that accent. However, it checks target.AccentDifficulty(accent, false) == Difficulty.Automatic instead of checking target.Accents.Contains(accent). AccentDifficulty is not an exact membership test: it starts from the accent's intrinsic difficulty or the stored familiarity and then lowers the difficulty based on other known accents in the same group. As a result, an accent with intrinsic Automatic difficulty, or one made Automatic by related-accent familiarity, can be skipped even though the character does not actually have that accent in _accents and it will not be persisted as learned.

title: Zone weather set command is not persisted
description: The commit adds `zone set <which> weather <wc|none>`, which assigns `zone.Weather` and marks the zone changed via the property setter. However, the zone persistence path saves other zone fields and timezones but omits `dbzone.WeatherControllerId = Weather?.Id`. Because zones are loaded from `zone.WeatherControllerId` on startup, any weather controller set or cleared with the new command will be lost after save/restart. This is a functional persistence bug, not a security vulnerability.

title: Namecultures prog collections use raw strings
description: Culture.namecultures and Ethnicity.namecultures are registered as ProgVariableTypes.Text | ProgVariableTypes.Collection, so FutureProg code can compile collection and text-item operations against them. However, GetProperty constructs CollectionVariable with a List<string>. CollectionVariable and collection extension functions generally expect collection elements to be IProgVariable instances; for example, .first/.last use 'as IProgVariable' and .reverse performs an explicit IProgVariable cast. As a result, some valid FutureProg expressions involving these new properties can return null unexpectedly or throw an InvalidCastException that is caught by the prog executor and reported as a prog error. This appears to be a functional/runtime bug rather than a security issue because FutureProg execution already catches these runtime exceptions in release builds, and access to prog editing/execution is staff-gated.

title: Corpse-eat emote validation accepts invalid $2 target
description: Race corpse-eating emotes are documented and executed with two perceivable references: $0 for the eater and $1 for the corpse/bodypart. The changed validation call now constructs an Emote with three dummy perceivables after the source, so validation will accept $2. At runtime, BodyNeeds constructs the Emote with only two perceivables, Actor and corpse.Parent or bodypart.Parent. A saved emote containing $2 will therefore become invalid when players eat corpses/bodyparts and will produce a bad emote/error output rather than the intended emote. I did not find a directly exploitable security vulnerability from this commit, because setting the emote requires Admin permission and the runtime invalid-emote path does not appear to grant privileges or expose sensitive data.

title: Liquid soak text edits are not persisted
description: The new sdescadd and descadd commands update the in-memory DampDescription, WetDescription, DrenchedDescription, DampShortDescription, WetShortDescription, and DrenchedShortDescription properties and mark the liquid as Changed. However, the Save() implementation updates many Liquid database columns but omits all six soak-description fields before clearing Changed. As a result, admins receive success messages and the values work only until the next save/reload/restart, after which the edits revert to the previous database values. This is a functional persistence bug rather than a security vulnerability because the command is Admin-gated and does not cross an authentication or privilege boundary.

title: Optional tags can crash repair kit seeding
description: UsefulSeeder now creates repair kit component XML by looking up required tag IDs in the private _tags dictionary. However, _tags is only populated by SeedTags, and SeedTags is still controlled by an independent optional question. If an operator answers yes to item package 4 but no to tags, the metal repair kit definitions dereference _tags["Armour"], _tags["Weapons"], or _tags["Tools"] while _tags is empty, causing a KeyNotFoundException and aborting seeding. This is a local operator-triggered availability/correctness bug in deployment tooling, not a remotely exploitable security vulnerability.

title: Board look multi-board output is malformed
description: The new BoardLook command only targets visible local board items and checks the board's CanViewBoard FutureProg before showing post titles. This does not create an evident authorization bypass or information leak beyond functionality already available through looking at a board item. However, in the multiple-board branch it interpolates the LINQ Select result directly into the output string, so players will see the enumerable type name instead of a list of boards.
